title: Suspected sex crimes: Tate brothers arrested in the US
embedding_text:
Title: Suspected sex crimes: Tate brothers arrested in the US

Suspicious sexual crimes Tate brothers arrested in the U.S. The controversial influencer Andrew Tate and his brother Tristan have been arrested in the U.S. Among other things, the brothers are accused of several sexual crimes. The British Public Prosecutor's Office has requested extradition. Andrew Tate and his brother Tristan Tate were arrested in the United States. The arrest took place in Miami on Saturday in connection with British police investigations into a number of suspected sexual crimes, as the Bedfordshire police reported. The brothers are scheduled to appear in front of a federal court in Miami next week, U.S. broadcaster CNN reported, citing law enforcement circles. Both are in custody According to British police, the two men were taken into custody during ongoing criminal proceedings. After an earlier investigation, it was first about the allegations of three victims. The British prosecutor's office has now announced that it will continue to press charges against the brothers, who are referring to accusations of four more victims. Among other things, it was about "rapeing, organizing or facilitating trafficking in human beings for the purpose of sexual exploitation and crimes related to child pornography," said Malcolm McHaffie, Head of the Special Crimes Department. Extradition is requested The alleged acts are said to have occurred between July 2010 and August 2017. According to the prosecutor's office, the two men were extradited. According to CNN, a brother's lawyer declared that the two were innocent and criticized the extradition requested. There was an agreement between the UK and the Romanian government that Britain would not apply for extradition while proceedings were still pending in Romania, Joseph McBride said. Law enforcement also in Romania Andrew and Tristan Tate are also prosecuted in Romania, and here too they dispute the allegations. They were to be extradited to the UK after the trial had been completed. The Tate brothers were born in the U.S., but moved to Luton north of London after their parents divorced their mother. They have US citizenship and British citizenship. At times, they had their focal point in Romania. Millions of people in social media Andrew Tate, a former kickboxer, reached millions of young people and young men with disdainful statements on social networks. His brother Tristan assisted him. The deputy police chief of Bedfordshire, Karena Thomas, said, "There is no room for male violence against women and girls." The police will continue to work tirelessly to support the victims and to investigate all reported incidents.

title: EU bans the destruction of unsold clothing - how effective is it?
embedding_text:
Title: EU bans the destruction of unsold clothing - how effective is it?

EU rules on unsold goods destroying clothes is forbidden - what good does it do? As of today, large companies can no longer simply destroy unsold clothes and shoes. The EU wants to conserve resources and reduce textile waste. But there is doubt as to whether this will succeed. Order online, try at home, send back - for many people, it's everyday life. However, what happens afterwards to the garments often remains invisible: some of the unsold goods are not resold but destroyed. The European Environment Agency (EEA) estimates that four to nine percent of all textile products on the market will be destroyed in Europe without ever being worn. Now the EU is pulling the rip line. With the Ecodesign Ordinance (ESPR), large companies are allowed from the 19th century onwards. In July 2026, unsold or returned clothes and shoes were no longer destroyed. Instead, the products should be reused, donated or recycled. The aim is to keep goods in circulation longer and not quickly end up in the garbage. Large exposure to textile waste In Germany alone, for example, around 17 million returned garments were disposed of in 2021. According to the EEA, up to 600,000 tons of unworn textiles land on the waste in Europe every year. This wastes resources and pollutes the environment and climate. The rules that are now in force are part of a broader strategy to make many products in the EU more durable, repairable and reusable, thus reducing CO2 and environmental footprint throughout the life cycle. The EU Ecodesign Regulation has been in force since the summer of 2024, and now the ban on textile destruction has been in effect for large companies. For medium-sized companies, longer transition periods will apply until 2030. Criticism of exceptions However, the ban is not quite clear. The Regulation provides for exceptions, for example where products cannot be reused for safety reasons or where re-use is technically inappropriate. This is criticised by environmental organisations. On the one hand, Michael Cieslik of Zero Waste Germany sceptical about the ban because it could fail in practice in terms of effort and costs. In his opinion, it would cost a lot of working time to check, sort, repack and re-sell returned clothes, so that some of the goods will probably be sorted out rather than used. It is also questionable whether unsold clothing can be donated sensibly, because there is often no fashion in demand here, but rather wearable clothing such as underwear: "It is not necessarily the case that every garment finds a buyer, and then the charitable associations hang on the old textiles, which they have to get rid of for disposal fees," he says. "So I hope that the big companies don't just shift the burden to others now." New regulation includes ambiguities Viola Wohlgemuth from Deutsche Umwelthilfe warns that too many exceptions could undermine the regulation. In addition, there is another problem: it is unclear how the ban is to be controlled. "When I find out that textiles are being further destroyed, I don't know who the competent authority is at first, where I can drop this information. And secondly, we don't have a fine catalogue. This means that even if we had this authority, she does not know with what punishment she can punish this offense." and the Bundesverband der Deutschen Sportartikel-Industrie e.V. Criticise also that robust data and realistic cost-benefit analyses were lacking. They call for industry to be closely involved in the design of the regulation.

title: Driver's license for many people priceless
embedding_text:
Title: Driver's license for many people priceless

Increased costs Driver's license too expensive for many young people The driver's license is the key to training, first job and own mobility for many young people. But in Germany it is becoming increasingly a luxury. Shortly after ten o'clock Jessica Köhler puts the Flex out of hand. Since six o'clock in the morning she has been working in a metal construction plant in Meissen, Saxony. The instructor is already waiting outside. The driving licence is indispensable for her profession - but she could not pay him alone. "4,000 euros is a pretty big sum. It's really hard to get that alone," says the trainee. Jessica is lucky: her business will cover the costs. The company invests around 9,000 euros in the driving licences of its two trainees. They cannot later be used on construction sites and assembly sites without a driving licence. Why driving licences are becoming more expensive The prices have risen massively over the past ten years. If the average cost of training in 2015 was around 2,100 euros, the total cost today is about 3,750 euros - an increase of around 40 percent adjusted for inflation. That's why more and more young people are moving their driving licence or giving up on it at first. For the price increase there are reasons, say the driving instructors: increasing costs for vehicles, fuel, insurances and workshops. At the same time, driving teachers are missing in many places. In addition, many driving students need more driving hours today than they did a few years ago. Every additional hour costs the training. Acquisition of driving licences should be made easier, more flexible and cheaper. Federal Transport Minister Patrick Schnieder wants to make driving licences easier, more flexible and cheaper. Digital theoretical offers, the increased use of driving simulators and changes in the mandatory special journeys are examined. But this is where the argument begins. While the Ministry is focusing on modernisation, the driver's associations warn against softening proven training standards. The crucial question is: can driving licences be made cheaper without endangering road safety? The search for answers leads to France. France: More practice, less costs Paris, 17. Arrondissement. Camille Maillard steers a clunky SUV safely through dense city traffic. Her father, Emanuele, sits next to her. What should surprise German drivers: The 17-year-old doesn't have a driver's license yet. "driving with my parents has given me much more confidence," she says. "I have experienced many different situations and am therefore better prepared to drive alone later on." What is hard to imagine in Germany has been part of the training system in France for decades. With the Conduite accompagnee model, young people from 15 years of age can collect 3,000 kilometres of driving practice with a registered accompanying person after at least 20 driving hours with a driving instructor.

title: Driver's license for many people priceless
embedding_text:
Title: Driver's license for many people priceless

With the Conduite accompagnee model, young people from 15 years of age can collect 3,000 kilometres of driving practice with a registered accompanying person after at least 20 driving hours with a driving instructor. Less driving hours thanks to practical experience The additional practice pays off: While in France a total of around 60 percent of the driving students pass the practical test, the success rate in accompanying driving increases to up to 75 percent. At the same time, many families save expensive driving hours because part of the training takes place privately. Camille's father also sees it as a security gain. "Parents want their children to be safe on the road," says Emanuele Maillard. "That's why we may even pay more attention to careful driving." This is exactly the core of the German reform debate: Can additional driving practice in everyday family life usefully complement classic driving training - or does it ultimately suffer from the quality of training? Between reform and safety concerns One thing is certain: the Federal Government wants to modernise driving training. More digital learning opportunities, the use of driving simulators and less mandatory special journeys are planned. The aim is to make the driver's license more flexible and cheaper. The driver's association sees the plans critically. Chairman Jürgen Kopp warns against softening proven training standards. Simulators were helpful, but could not realistically convey dangerous traffic situations. Even when driving with the parents, the association fears that mistakes and unfavourable driving habits will become firmer. Bundesverkehrsminister Schnieder is opposed. Many reforms have long been proven in other European countries. The safety argument should not fundamentally prevent necessary changes. Compromise model from Austria? However, the view to France shows a differentiated picture. Young drivers are more likely to be fatal there than in Germany. However, experts do not attribute this to driving training alone. The main causes are more serious road accidents, differences in infrastructure and traffic behaviour as well as the higher proportion of motorised two-wheelers. Austria shows that there is no need for a more favourable and certainly no contradiction. There, accompanied driving is closely intertwined with professional driving training. Young people collect additional driving practice with their parents, but remain regularly connected to the driving school. For many experts, this is a viable compromise. Also important for the business location What the reform will look like in the end is open. The only thing that is certain is that the pressure for action is growing. For many young people - especially in the countryside - the driver's license decides whether they can start training, reach a job or even be mobile. If the driver's license becomes priceless for more and more people, this not only affects driving students - but also companies and the business location Germany. The question is therefore no longer whether driving training will be reformed, but how much change Germany will allow.

title: Problem case General renovations: Do railways and politics have a plan?
embedding_text:
Title: Problem case General renovations: Do railways and politics have a plan?

Problem case General renovations Do railways and politics have a plan? The broken rail network should be repaired piece by piece. However, there were often problems with renovations. Transport Minister Schnieder refers to the railway - but that does not let everyone go through it. "Today we have good news for our passengers," announced rail manager Evelyn Palla just in Berlin - but only the announced communication offensive of the company was meant. When it comes to the renewal of important railway lines, the negative headlines are increasing: just like this week in North Rhine-Westphalia, when the Cologne-Wuppertal line went back into operation despite a long renovation with major problems. The idea behind the so-called general renovations at the start two years ago sounded very promising: Instead of screwing around on tracks or interlockings forever, it was better to shut everything down for a few months - and then have a long rest, too, the politicians hoped. "We want several years of construction freedom on these routes, we want to get them right on track and do as much as possible as possible," explains Federal Transport Minister Patrick Schnieder (CDU) at Politico. And must admit: "We have not achieved all the goals we have previously agreed on." Minister points his finger at the train The best example is Hamburg-Berlin: Even nine months of full lock-up were not enough in the end to release the route after the construction work last spring on time. At first, the railways have also renounced the promised digitalisation, although this will be mandatory in the EU in the future. Further roadblocks are therefore already foreseeable in the coming years - another broken promise. In total, the 40 planned large-scale construction sites in the railway network are now to extend until the mid- 2030s. "Now the train has to give us clear information: Why is that? How do we do this better next time? Or do we have to change something in the concept as a whole?", warns Schnieder. The Minister has been pointing his finger on the track for a long time in the event of problems. But Dirk Flege won't let him get away with it. The managing director of the Pro-Rail Alliance sees the federal government as the owner in the obligation: "This is not a task with which you can leave the DB alone. That's tax money that's being installed here, and the minister has announced a review. Now he also has to stay in the control seat and control this inspection."Ministry wants to hold on to full lock-ups This test also involves passenger associations and freight transport, which suffers particularly from the months-long construction sites on the rail network. Christian Böttger, railway expert at the Hochschule für Technik und Wirtschaft Berlin, gives little hope here: "I do not see that the ministry or the railway have a serious new plan. But I believe that we need to think about the rules. For example, it is harder for us than in other countries to make single-track operations. That is to say, that it is built on one side and can operate on the other." This is now to be partially possible with the upcoming renovation between Berlin and Hanover from October. The next mega construction site to be completed at the end of 2027 - as of today. In principle, the Federal Transport Minister wants to stick to the concept of full locks. Schnieder, on the other hand, puts other questions at the centre of the discussion: "Is this the case with four corridor renovations a year? Don't we have to distribute them more regionally? Do we need to take a closer look at the effects of this?"

title: World Cup Final: A half-time show like the Super Bowl
embedding_text:
Title: World Cup Final: A half-time show like the Super Bowl

World Cup Final A half-time show like the Super Bowl For the first time there will be a half-time show according to the US model in a final of the World Cup. If Shakira, Madonna, Justin Bieber and other stars appear today, the prescribed 15-minute break could be clearly overlaid. FIFA boss Gianni Infantino wants to prove what the Super Bowl final can do in American football: The mid-term show of the Football World Cup is supposed to surpass that of the Super Bowl. Stars like Madonna, Shakira and BTS So when the pros walk into their cabins after the half-time whistle, Shakira, Madonna, BTS, Justin Bieber and many other superstars conquer the lawn. Shakira will probably sing her World Cup song "Dai Dai". And the South Korean Boygroup BTS will welcome billions of TV viewers with their song "The World Unite" to the "biggest stage in the world". What Madonna and Justin Bieber contribute remains a secret. Also the performances of the Venezuelan conductor Gustavo Dudamel, a New York primary school choir and the Muppet characters Kermit and Miss Piggy. Coldplay singer Chris Martin, as artistic director, has the difficult task of placing all the superstars in 15 minutes. "Billions of people will watch" In any case, it will be "the most seen event in history," announces Hugh Evans, head of organization of the half-time show in the ARD interview: "Billions and billions of people will watch. 31 times as many as the Super Bowl. The whole world should share." But isn't that going to blow up the 15-minute half-time break? Like the finale of the club World Cup a year ago, when the half-time show with pop stars lasted almost half an hour. Much longer, at least, than is provided for in the rules of football. Is the half-time interval maintained? Only with the arbitrator's permission may the half-term period exceptionally be longer. But head of organization Hugh Evans promises: "The half-time show will only take eleven minutes. We will be absolutely respectful of football. We'll cover the place so that the lawn is spared. The eleven minutes will be the most entertaining eleven minutes of the story." Spectacle to serve a good purpose In addition, the great half-time spectacle serves a good purpose, says Organisation Director Hugh Evans. He leads the aid organization Global Citizen, which organizes a charity concert in New York's Central Park every year to fight hunger and poverty. The half-time show will be "the biggest collection of artists for a good cause since the Life Aid concert in 1985", Evans emphasizes. The half-time show is to bring in 100 million US dollars: for an education fund that allows children in poorer countries to attend school and play football. That would help everyone: FIFA, the superstars, the aid organization and hopefully also the children.

title: New attacks between USA and Iran
embedding_text:
Title: New attacks between USA and Iran

After the death of two US soldiers New attacks between the US and Iran The US has again reported air attacks on Iran - Iran responded with attacks on two US military bases in Kuwait. Previously, two U.S. soldiers had been killed in Iranian fire in Jordan. After the death of two of his soldiers, the U.S. military has reported new air strikes against Iran. The responsible regional commando CENTCOM informed X that the attacks were aimed at "immediately punishing" the Iranian Revolutionary Guard. In addition, Iran's ability "to threaten commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormus" should be further weakened. Rocket strikes were reported near the island of Gheshm in the Persian Gulf. The Iranian army responded with drone attacks on two US military bases in Kuwait. This was a reaction to the US attacks, according to the armed forces. Soldiers killed in Jordan Previously, the US had reported dead soldiers after Iranian fire for the first time since the early days of the war. They were killed on Friday in an operation in Jordan when US forces and allies defended themselves against Iranian missile and threat attacks. Another soldier is still missing. The officially known number of US soldiers killed in the Iran war thus rises to 16. This is likely to further increase the pressure on US President Donald Trump. Iran also stated on Saturday that the framework agreement with the US no longer existed. "Unfortunately, the Americans have violated their obligations under Islamabad's declaration of intent with their aggressive measures," Far's Deputy Foreign Minister Kasem Gharibabadi quoted. That was why Iran no longer feels bound by the agreement.

Title: Again massive Russian rocket attack on Kiev

War against Ukraine Once again massive Russian rocket attack on Kiev Russia attacked Ukraine's capital Kiev again with ballistic missiles at night. According to the authorities, impacts were reported in several districts. According to information, at least one person was killed, several were injured. The Ukrainian capital Kiev has again been massively attacked by the Russian military with ballistic missiles. Dozens of explosions were heard at night. According to the authorities, at least one person was killed. 13 other people had been injured. Russia has fired about four dozen of these missiles, Foreign Minister Andriy Sybiha wrote on the online platform X. He described the Russian action as a brutal terrorist attack and called for appropriate and strong reactions and more pressure on the leadership in Moscow to put an end to this terror. According to the Ukrainian Air Force, Russia had deployed 41 rockets during the night attack. The air defense intercepted 18 missiles. The main objective of the Russian attack was the Ukrainian capital Kiev. Impacts in several neighborhoods According to the latest data from the Ukrainian police, six urban districts were hit during the night attacks. In the capital, fires broke out, among other things, in a dorm, a house and a supermarket, informed Mayor Vitali Klitschko. Ukraine has been resisting the Russian invasion for more than four years. The Ukrainian army recently significantly intensified its drone attacks on Russia. According to the authorities, eight people were killed in attacks on two logistics centres in Russia on Saturday. The Ukrainian port city of Odessa reported at least two deaths as a result of Russian attacks. Selenskyy reacts to protests Meanwhile, protests against the dismissal of Defense Minister Mychaylo Fedorov were again taking place in Ukraine. In response, President Wolodymyr indicated Selenskyj changes in the army leadership. "There were many discussions yesterday and today. Of course, I hear what people say," Selenskyj said in a video message in the evening. He had spoken "long" with Fedorov and with army chief Olexander Syrskyj. title: Hungary's President paves the way for his own resignation
embedding_text:
Title: Hungary's President paves the way for his own resignation

Law signed Hungary's President clears the way for his own dismissal With a constitutional amendment, Hungary's new government wants to get rid of President Sulyok, who is considered a confidant of Orban. Sulyok had hesitated for days - but now agreed to the change, which means his own cancellation. The new pro-European ruling party Tisza has a two-thirds majority in the Hungarian parliament and can therefore change the constitution. Last Monday, she decided on the 17th. Constitutional amendment, including the resignation of President Tamas Sulyok. The allegation that he was only a puppet of former Prime Minister Viktor Orban, had supported the corrupt authoritarian system and just signed all laws for Orban. After the parliament decided on Monday Sulyok's resignation, the president had until this Saturday to sign the law - but for a long time he seemed to refuse. Then, however, he got involved and explained himself in a video on his Facebook page early Saturday night. He called the constitutional amendment a "exemplary process" in 36 years of Hungarian democracy. The President and the Constitutional Court cannot protect the Hungarian Constitution, as the Constitution does not provide for it. Parliament is the constitutional or constitutional authority. After careful consideration of my legal possibilities and my conscience, I now fulfil my duty as laid down in the Constitution. My signature is the last seal of my duty as President. Magyar with appeal to the citizens The Hungarian Prime Minister Peter Magyar also spoke immediately, with a video on his social media channels. He stated that Sulyok's term as president was ended at 0.30 a.m. on Monday, and then the President of Parliament Agnes Forsthoffer would temporarily take over. Then Parliament has 30 days to elect a new president. Magyar appealed to the citizens to participate in the presidential search. Let's decide together. We don't want to determine the best candidate in backrooms or cigar clubs. Proposals should come from everywhere: from parties, from representatives of the public, from civil society actors and from private individuals. Tell us who they think is worthy of representing the Hungarian Republic and the Hungarian people on the basis of what values. Restricted terms of office of parliamentarians In the long term, the Tisza party plans that the president will be directly elected by the people. A new constitution for Hungary will be drawn up in the autumn. Until then, there are changes to the current constitution. The resignation of President Sulyok is just one example. The term of office of parliamentarians was also limited to twelve years and that of the Prime Minister to eight years. Orban is not allowed to come back to power because he was prime minister for 20 years in total. It was also decided to set up an authority to recover embezzled taxpayers' money and EU aid. Public service broadcasting, which was an Orban propaganda machine, is completely reorganized by a new media law. Magyar's survey values rise Orban's Fidesz party protests loudly and speaks of autocratic methods. Lawyers from Germany and abroad urge caution to remain within the legal framework and not to overstretch the bow. Meanwhile, the survey figures for the new Prime Minister Peter Magyar are increasing. Thus, up to 70 percent of Hungarians are satisfied with the fact that Magyar consistently clean up the Orban system.

title: Norwegian archipelago: Lofoten levy levy against "Overtourism"

Title: Norwegian archipelago: Lofoten levy levy against "Overtourism"

Norwegian archipelago Lofoten levy levy against "Overtourism" Overcrowded parking spaces, campers on fields, garbage on the roadside: the Lofoten groan for years among too many tourists. A tourist tax is intended to remedy the situation - but not all consider the fee to be effective. When farmer Anders Nilsen drives to his field in the summer, he has a thought every time: Are my plants still standing, or have they been flattened again by campers? His field is right across from a popular beach on the Lofoten: "If I don't have a sign in front of the driveway, there are suddenly 100 or 200 caravans on my field. People don't think the area is being used. Then they just drive on it and camp. They destroy the food for our animals." Too many campers: On the Lofoten, the anger about the growing tourism is great. "In the past, only a few German tourists came" Hardly any region stands for the tourist boom as much as the Lofoten. More than one million visitors come to the archipelago every year - with just about 25,000 inhabitants. Especially in the summer months, roads, parking spaces and hiking trails regularly reach their limits. On hot summer days there are regularly chaotic conditions in front of his fields, says Bauer Nilsen. Motorhomes piled up on the narrow streets, cars blocked alternates. "In the past, only a few German tourists came here. Today, people come from all over the world." No getting through: in summer, so many people want to experience nature on the Lofoten that it gets too tight on the streets and parking lots. Municipalities have been demanding a solution for years That municipalities are now allowed to levy a tax on tourism is the result of a long political debate. After years of discussion, the Norwegian Parliament passed a law on this this year. It allows municipalities particularly heavily affected by tourism to introduce a tourist tax. They can additionally charge up to three percent of the overnight stay. Farmer Anders Nilsen has already experienced that caravans are standing on his field and destroying the plants. "We will build more toilets" However, the amount will probably only be raised from January 2027, because the municipalities have to develop concepts to prove that the revenues flow exclusively into tourist infrastructure. This is an important step for many municipalities on the Lofoten. So far, they have had to cut most of this expenditure out of their own households. In the municipality of Vågan, additional toilets were recently installed on popular hiking trails. Further investments are to follow thanks to the tourist tax, the deputy mayor Marit Olsen says: "We will build more toilets.

On the other hand, if you stay with the camper for free on parking places or camp wildly, you do not have to pay the fee at first. This is precisely what many municipalities see as a weakness of the law. Especially campers and wild campers caused high loads in many places, without them participating in the costs. Parts of the hotel industry are also critical of the new regulation. They fear competitive disadvantages because of the burden on the guests who already book regular accommodation and thus spend money in the regions anyway. Between hospitality and excessive demand The region benefits significantly economically from tourism. Hotels, restaurants and providers of outdoor activities live by the guests. Hotel owner Ola Skjeseth therefore welcomes visitors from all over the world. His holiday accommodation was created from former fishermen's huts. "We on the Lofoten have learned to be good hosts," he says. In the meantime, however, the number of visitors had increased so much that the infrastructure could no longer keep up. "If you want to experience this exclusive and beautiful place, you should pay a contribution." Dispute over everyone's right For many locals, it is no longer just about money. They observe that some visitors misunderstand Norwegian everyone's right. This allows in principle to move freely in nature and to camp under certain conditions, but at the same time commits to respect for nature and property. "Many tourists in Norwegian hardly know a word. But they all know everyone's right," says Bauer Anders Nilsen. "They believe that this right allows them everything. But it was never meant for tourists to be able to do whatever they want." With the new tourist tax, Norway now hopes to better manage the balancing act between growing tourism and the protection of its nature. Title: Debate on surrogacy: What the spade withdrawal means for the CDU

Debate on surrogacy What the spahn withdrawal means for the CDU The pressure on the debate about surrogacy became too strong - Spahn has resigned. Before that, there was massive criticism from our own camp and concerns about the election campaign. What does this mean for the CDU, the state elections and the coalition? First came the congratulations, then the resignation wishes. But what ultimately made the decision that Jens Spahn actually resigned? The Head of Group had lost support for all relevant people in the Union. In the group, many Members were apparently displeased because Spahn had completely surprised them with his communication about his husband's paternity with a surrogate mother. And they were stunned that Spahn has allowed himself in the USA something that is forbidden in Germany. In addition, the CDU had again taken a decision on the party congress in February, which strongly rejects surrogacy. The fear was great that the credibility of the CDU was severely damaged by Spahn. No support for Spahn The CDU chairman in Mecklenburg-Vorpommern and several national associations of the Women's Union already demanded Spahn's resignation on Friday. At the latest, Chancellor Friedrich Merz was also aware that the discussion about Spahn would continue - a debate that came to an untimely conclusion for the Chancellor. Shortly before a summer interview and at the beginning of a summer break, the chancellor wanted to go with a positive message: reforms united, coalition capable of action. No prominent Christian democrat and no one from the CSU spoke as supporters of Spahn. Instead, resignation demands even came from the Kreisverband von Friedrich Merz - so it was clear that the resignation was inevitable. CDU campaigners in difficult positions In addition, the CDU is in the election campaign in three federal states. In Berlin, Saxony-Anhalt and Mecklenburg-Vorpommern it was already under strong pressure. In Berlin, the CDU had just cleared up its own credibility crisis around Mayor Kai Wegner. The three associations could not use a week-long discussion about Jens Spahn, too. First, the campaigners must defend the reforms of the coalition at every level of information. Some of them hit people in the East harder than in the West, because there are, among other things, lower wages. Then if someone like Spahn can afford a surrogate mother with a lot of money, it works far away from the problems of ordinary citizens. Secondly, the expectation of the CDU there remains high that it stands on its conservative principles and decisions. And thirdly, of course, the political opponents of the CDU would have taken advantage of the accusation of double morale against Spahn. There's something that you can't do to others. This made it clear that the faster Spahn withdraws, the better for the elections in autumn.

Apparently, he had insisted that the party and faction also consider this to be private, the contradiction to German laws and CDU party resolutions. And indeed, in the first round, the public reactions of party friends and group colleagues were initially positive. Nevertheless, many in the Union quickly realized that the credibility of the politician Jens Spahn was shaken - to the detriment of the CDU. Reactions not predictable? The fact that Spahn underestimated these reactions also shows his resignation letter. There he writes: "The gap between my private decision to become a child through surrogacy and the understandable expectation of me as chairman of our group" has become greater than I expected." Spahn could have foreseen the indignation in Christian-conservative circles. For already last year Spahn had undermined a similar misjudgement. He had underestimated the rejection that the positions of the constitutional judge candidate Frauke Brosius-Gersdorf on abortion would meet with Christian-conservative core voters of the Union. Even then, party friends had wondered how the otherwise strategically clever Spahn could overlook this. New Group leader must rebuild confidence Who could lead the group in the future and close the gap that leaves Spahn's resignation? In Berlin, various names are mentioned, including Thorsten Frei, CDU, who is currently head of the Chancellery. For this purpose, Günter Krings, CDU, currently chairman of the NRW Landesgruppe in the Union faction, could move to the Chancellery. CDU CEO Friedrich Merz and CSU CEO Markus Söder have the right to propose the group presidency. Commissionerly, CSU head Alexander Hoffmann will lead the group until then. The question remains whether the constructive mode of work that the coalition has found in recent months will continue to work with a new group leader. Spahn had established a good relationship with its chairman Matthias Miersch, with which the reform compromises of the past months were also possible. All of this will have to be rebuilt by the new one at the head of the faction.

title: Artificial Intelligence: Expulse AI bands from real musicians?
embedding_text:
Title: Artificial Intelligence: Expulse AI bands from real musicians?

Artificial Intelligence Expulse AI bands for real musicians? AI-generated songs flood the streaming services. More and more often, there are also bands created by artificial intelligence, with their own biography and avatars. The end of creativity - or a chance? "We don't water God's Word, we spread it." That's the motto of the band Shunned at a Funeral. So it is in the band description on the official YouTube channel of the band. In the tape photo, a three women look seriously at you. The Christian rock band has around 250,000 subscribers there. From music videos to live tracks to videos that show the band in the studio, the homepage contains everything that belongs to the usual portfolio. Only: Shunned at a Funeral does not actually exist. Or rather, they are not a band of flesh and blood. If you want to, you can see it. The music videos often look like computer games. In the video for the song "Twirl" the band members walk with a little girl through a softly drawn nature. The light, the shadows, everything is atmospheric and perfectly set - a bit too perfect maybe. Several hundred thousand calls to Spotify The enormous musical output of seven albums in 2025 and three albums in 2026 also suggests AI support. How much is this used in the creation of the songs? It's hard to say. The band's website merely refers to the use of modern tools in the creative process, but the heart of the project is human. On Spotify, the songs sometimes have several hundred thousand calls, most comments among the music videos are positive. Either the listeners don't notice that Shunned at a Funeral is behind artificial intelligence (AI), or they simply don't care. This is not unusual, says Ali Nikrang, professor of artificial intelligence and musical creation at the Musikhochschule in Munich. Although products that are completely AI-generated would be critically received. But if behind the AI mission is an artistic concept conceived by a human being, then the audience appreciates the results again as in other works of art, says Nikrang. What is creativity? Chopping a prompt into an AI tool and then putting the result on Spotify is not yet a creative act, says Nikrang, but: "If you make an entire band, where all the characters are virtual and try to ask, how does it matter to the audience, then I would say, okay, there's something new about it, that's a creative engagement with the AI." Daniel Troha also posed the question of what matters to the audience when he considered a vita for an AI artist named Kai Nøir. He then chose a young Scandinavian producer.

title: Artificial Intelligence: Expulse AI bands from real musicians?
embedding_text:
Title: Artificial Intelligence: Expulse AI bands from real musicians?

He then chose a young Scandinavian producer. "Such a young guy who likes to sit in the studio and screw on some analog devices. That's what makes minimal tech house music, that was my idea." A label only for AI artists As a music producer, Troha produced songs with Sarah Connor or the No Angels. Kai Nøir releases his music on Smopp Records, a label for AI artists only, whose managing director is Troha. "We actually do exactly what the other labels do, just that we offer the platform for AI-produced or generated music." The label operates very classical artist structure and thus tries to establish its own artists on the market - i.e. to make them visible on Spotify. As a musician Troha can understand the criticism of the use of AI. But he feels it "should be that through these tools people can implement their kind of creativity, which may not be able to play an instrument or who may not be able to afford a recording studio or even have the money for singers or musicians."When AI meets reality The AI tool as a tool to realize artistic ideas. That's how Troha sees it. However, he does not believe that AI artists make real artists obsolete. "I think these are parallel worlds that will find coexistence." What can happen if these two parallel worlds are linked together is currently being experienced by US rapper Fenix Flexin. He made quite successful hip-hop until he released the song "Rubberz" a few weeks ago. Because the number no longer sounds like a 90s trap-hip-hop, but sounds like soft-washed 80s synth pop and Flexin no longer raps with a broad American slang, but sings with a British accent, the net quickly made the accusation that the song came from the pen of an AI. Since then, Flexin has been eagerly denying and pointing out that he also sings the song live. However, the videos of these appearances are not suitable to remove doubts. Because the performance is rather mixed: Flexin and his song "Rubberz" seem to each other like foreign bodies. However, a lesson for the AI age can definitely be drawn from this case: Maybe it is no longer a problem for the audience if AI helps to write songs. One should only admit it if it is so.

title: Political scientist on Spahn: "Spahn underestimated the topic"
embedding_text:
Title: Political scientist on Spahn: "Spahn underestimated the topic"

Political scientist in the interview "Spahn has underestimated the topic" Political scientist Oliver Lembcke in the tagesschau24 interview about the reasons for Spahn's resignation - and what consequences this could have. tagesschau24: Mr. Lembcke, did Jens Spahn's resignation come in time or has the CDU already suffered damage to credibility? Lembcke: The whole thing developed very quickly. Within a very short time, a kind of protest tsunami has built up, which has certainly come very quickly for the CDU. However, the problem has, of course, been upstream. It would have been up to Spahn, for example, to make it clear at the Federal Party Congress of the CDU at the beginning of the year in Stuttgart that he deviates from the CDU program with his life planning. And that might have been the opportunity to open up a room for discussion. So, however, he has been virtually washed away by this wave of protest within his own party. tagesschau24: Spahn has rejected the legalization of surrogacy in the past. But what is forbidden in Germany, he and his husband handled through surrogacy in the USA. Did Spahn seriously believe he could get away with it? Lembcke: I would think yes. I suspect that this is a form of political instinctlessness. He has fought back in his role as leader of the political group, has become the stabiliser of the federal government and has had an important role in which he has increasingly been a centre of power of his own. I believe that it was very difficult for him to imagine that this issue could actually cause him these difficulties. This socio-politically controversial question is simply a central and important issue for Christian Democrats, on which many do not want to shake. And that is why, I think, he has greatly underestimated the whole issue. tagesschau24: Is it possible at all that a politician, especially in the function of group chairman, separates between himself as a politician and as a private person? Lembcke: There may be cases where such a thing is conceivable. It is of course a very difficult ridge hike. But in this case, I'd say that's the classic of politics. This creates exactly the credibility problem that Jens Spahn also had. You can agree with all people that they learn and that they change their minds. But then they have to deal with it transparently and address it at the right time. That is exactly what Spahn did not do, but at first he gave the impression that the rules that politics makes are not for him, he takes it from it. tagesschau24: The CDU, faces major challenges.

title: Framework Agreement: Iran suspends agreement with USA

Title: Framework Agreement: Iran suspends agreement with USA

Agreement with the US Iran no longer feels bound to agreements Actually, the US and Iran had agreed in mid-June to resolve their conflict through negotiations. But now the situation escalates again. Two U.S. soldiers were killed in an Iranian attack in Jordan. According to the government in Tehran, the framework agreement between the US and Iran no longer exists. The agreement was suspended, said Deputy Foreign Minister Kasem Gharibabadi, according to a report from the Fars news agency. "We are busy defending the country," the agency quoted the minister from a TV interview. "Unfortunately, the Americans have violated their obligations under Islamabad's declaration of intent with their aggressive measures," Gharibabadi continued. That was why Iran no longer feels bound by the agreement. The US and Iran had actually reached a framework agreement in mid-June to pave the way for a lasting end to the war. Framework Agreement: Deal within 60 days In it was agreed to negotiate a final deal within 60 days. It also includes the demand for an opening of the street of Hormus. A ceasefire has also been in place since the beginning of April. At the beginning of July, however, the conflict was escalated again. Second US soldiers killed Two US soldiers have been killed for the first time since the recent escalation in the Iran war, according to the US military. They were killed on Friday in an operation in Jordan when US forces and allies defended themselves against Iranian missile and drone attacks. This was announced by the responsible CENTCOM Regional Command on Platform X. Another soldier is also missing. In addition, several soldiers were injured during the attack.

title: Several potential candidates for the Presidency of the Union Group
embedding_text:
Title: Several potential candidates for the Presidency of the Union Group

President of the Union Who could follow Spahn? The head of the Union faction must be re-occupied. Chancellor Merz apparently does not want to lose time - and according to the personal reports, the media will clarify before the holiday period begins. There are several potential candidates. After the mask affair Jens Spahn had caught himself again, now a private decision brought him down. He and his husband had decided to become parents with the help of a surrogate mother from the United States. In Germany surrogacy is not allowed - and Spahn's party is strictly against legalization. He, too, had spoken out against it in the past. The pressure on the 46-year-old CDU politician has continued to grow in recent days, including in his own party. Now he has declared his resignation. The Union therefore stands there for the time being without the head of the group and must take care of its successor. Who is eligible for the post? Thorsten Frei, Head of Chancellery (CDU) He is considered a top candidate for the leadership of the faction. During the last parliamentary term, Frei was the parliamentary director at the side of the former leader of the faction Friedrich Merz. After the winning Bundestag election, he took him to the chancellor's office. Free, however, already made it clear at that time that he would have liked to become the head of the Union's political group. If he was to take over, Merz would have to look for a new chancellor. Alexander Dobrindt, Federal Minister of the Interior (CSU) Dobrindt already has experience in leading a faction. He was chairman of the CSU Land Group in the Bundestag for eight years, including in the period of Angela Merkel's black and red coalition. He was already CSU Secretary General, Federal Minister of Transport and has been Interior Minister in the current Federal Government since May 2025 and would also break a big gap with a change. Carsten Linnemann, Secretary General (CDU) From 2022 to 2024 he was deputy chairman of the CDU. Since July 2023, he has been Secretary General of the Party. Merz had offered him to become a member of the cabinet, but Linnemann had decided against it. In a message, the party calls him a "driver, explainer and impulse giver". Nina Warken, Federal Minister of Health (CDU) Warken is a member of the Women's Union and since May 2025 Federal Minister of Health. Before that, she was Secretary General of the CDU in Baden-Württemberg. It is currently in focus mainly because of reforms and cuts in health care. Chancellor Merz attested her courage, prudence and empathy in the context. At Warken, too, a new ministerial post would have to be filled if it were to move to the top of the faction. Michael Brand, Parliamentary State Secretary at the Ministry of Family Affairs (CDU) According to Bild-Zeitung, he is also in the race for the position of group chairman. He is currently Parliamentary State Secretary at the Ministry of Family Affairs. Brand writes on his website that he was asked by Merz at the time to take over the post in the ministry. Before his resignation, he had accused Spahn of a "morally clear breach of law" and had spoken of a "real imposition". Günter Krings, Deputy Head of Union Political Group (CDU) According to a report by the Spiegel, Krings could become a possible successor of Frei in the Chancellery. It seems that he is also acting as a possible leader of the political group. He is currently deputy head of the Union's political group. In December 2025, he ran for the chair of the Konrad Adenauer Foundation as a candidate for Chancellor Merz. However, he failed in a fight vote to Annegret Kramp-Karrenbauer.

Title: Reactions to the resignation of Jens Spahn as President of the Union Group

Reactions to Spahn's resignation "Right", "Unavoidable", "long overdue" After Spahn's resignation as Head of the Union Group, there is support from the coalition. The opposition calls the step "overdue" and denounces a "double morale" of the CDU politician. Party colleagues and members of the Federal Government have paid respect to Jens Spahn for his decision to resign as head of the Union political group. CDU chief and chancellor Friedrich Merz called the step "right" and "unavoidable". "Faithfulness is the highest asset in politics. I thank Jens Spahn for the cooperation," Merz said. According to Merz, the CDU politician had shaped and shaped the group's path from the opposition to the government. The deputy CDU chairman Karin Prien also thanked Spahn "for the good and trusting cooperation". The family minister wrote on Platform X, this step in the service of the credibility of Christian democracy deserves respect. "For him and his family, I sincerely wish you all the best and all the best." CSU Head of Country Group: "Contributing significantly to the success of this coalition" CSU Head Markus Söder said that this was "a personal decision" and that he paid respect for it. "I thank Jens Spahn for the very good cooperation, especially in difficult times." Praise and recognition also came from the CSU head of state Alexander Hoffmann. "The decision of Jens Spahn deserves the utmost respect," said Hoffmann der ARD. Spahn had done excellent work as a group chairman and had been a "stability factor" for the coalition. The chairmen of CDU and CSU, Friedrich Merz and Markus Söder, would now take the time to decide on a suitable candidate for Spahn's successor. Until the election of the new chairman of the political group, he would take over the office, continued Hoffmann. SPD Group thanks for trusting cooperation SPD Group Chairman Matthias Miersch said that he and Spahn had "very close and trustful cooperation" in the coalition. On the political assessment of the events, he did not say that this was a question that the Union had to clarify with itself. The coalition must continue to work together. Also Miersch wished Spahn and his family all the best.

title: Government plans to cut red tape: businesses remain skeptical
embedding_text:
Title: Government plans to cut red tape: businesses remain skeptical

Reform to reduce bureaucracy Scepticism instead of euphoria in the middle class The government wants to reduce bureaucracy with the "Programme for Recovery and Employment". But the problem is only partly at federal level. The findings have long been known: bureaucracy acts as a serious brake on growth. Marcel Thum, head of the ifo branch in Dresden, estimates the cost of the bureaucracy in Germany at about 150 billion euros per year. Added to this is the loss of value added - a significant part of working time must be spent on documentation and reporting obligations. Is the Recovery and Employment Programme the solution? With the "Programme for Recovery and Employment", the Federal Government wants to boost the economy and to significantly reduce bureaucracy. It is the repeated attempt to tackle a particularly persistent problem. Among other things, a flat-rate abolition of certain reporting obligations, changes to the works representatives and the extension of the so-called approval fiction are envisaged. Another point is the mandatory safety tests for electrical installations and equipment. This verification obligation is now to be "reworked" and "simplified". Safety tests "completely coated" In the knitting factory of Gerald Rosner in Apolda there are about 2,000 plugs and devices, which have to be checked regularly by an electrician and then get a test badge. "For our company, where there is hardly any mechanical wear and tear in the equipment, this is completely overworked," says Rosner. If the obligation were removed or significantly relaxed, it could save costs, would have capacity for other orders - and the products could be offered more cheaply overall. Companies complain about the burden of regulations Hehnke GmbH in Thuringia also feels the burden of regulations. The company with around 100 employees produces special plastic parts. Torsten Herrmann, Managing Director of Hehnke GmbH, shows the fire safety regulations of his company: eleven pages of paper. In addition, there are numerous operating instructions - for handling hand knives, compressed air tools, load absorbers and cranes. "We're all skilled workers. You might think no one knows what he's doing. That's why there are all these operating instructions again. And of course, these must be kept up to date, above all," says Herrmann. In addition, there are testing requirements for roller doors, shelves, ladders. Every check must be documented.

title: Government plans to cut red tape: businesses remain skeptical
embedding_text:
Title: Government plans to cut red tape: businesses remain skeptical

That's why there are all these operating instructions again. And of course, these must be kept up to date, above all," says Herrmann. In addition, there are testing requirements for roller doors, shelves, ladders. Every check must be documented. In addition, the company is obliged to appoint and train company representatives on a regular basis. Obligation to appoint business representatives to be abolished The Federal Government now wants to partially abolish this obligation to appoint business representatives. Mr. Herrmann, who is also President of the IHK Südthuringen, considers this to be a correct step. The extension of the so-called authorisation fiction was also a good idea. This concerns the general assumption that an application is automatically approved if the Authority does not object within four months. This could make construction projects much easier, says Herrmann. But the core of the bureaucracy remains untouched. "There is only talk of relieving the federal laws. However, much of our burden today comes from the European Union," he warns. EU requirements make red tape more difficult As EU rules increase expenditure, a thumb-sized component shows. It is made of plastic, a metal shaft and a magnet. Torsten Herrmann explains that the suppliers of the pre-products, such as the magnet, must request the exact composition, and the information on this must be documented in detail. For a medium-sized company this is a "huge effort". Such documentation obligations are often linked to EU chemicals or product safety legislation. The Federal Government cannot simply abolish them in its national reform programme. Many companies therefore call for a reduction of bureaucracy at European level - otherwise a large part of the burden will remain. All non-EU reporting obligations should be eliminated The government is still presenting its programme as a big throw. Key point: All reporting obligations not required by EU law should be eliminated at a flat rate. Those who wish to request further reports should have to re-examine them. Federal Chancellor Friedrich Merz (CDU) says: "If a ministry wants to obtain reporting obligations, it must re-justify this and fight for the continuation of the old reporting obligations." However, from the point of view of Marcel Thum from the ifo Institute, this approach carries risks. It could be "very, very, very difficult to make clear what is necessary and what really needs to fall away urgently." The success of the reform programme thus depends essentially on how consistently ministries do not give up what is desired - or yet defend it.

title: Debate on surrogacy: Union leader Spahn resigns
embedding_text:
Title: Debate on surrogacy: Union leader Spahn resigns

Debate on surrogacy Union leader Spahn resigns In the debate on surrogacy in the United States, Jens Spahn draws conclusions: in a letter he informed the Union faction about his resignation. Chancellor Merz described the decision as "right and inevitable". Jens Spahn resigns in the debate on surrogacy as the head of the Union's political group. He had become aware that his personal happiness in founding a family was incompatible with his political office, says Spahn's letter to the CDU/CSU faction, which is before the ARD-Hauptstadtstudio. "The balancing act between my private decision to become a child through surrogacy and the understandable expectation of me as chairman of our group" had become bigger than he expected. "Let us remain human in the tone" The increasing inexorability in the public debate has made him very thoughtful, the letter continues - coupled with a request: "Let us always remain human in the tone with all clarity and determination in the matter." The Polish President's veto activities are slowing down the government. The party of Prime Minister Donald Tusk has even dedicated a website to this process that lists the vetoes of Karol Nawrocki. The head of state is referred to as "vetomat" there: as someone who seems to stop the laws as they are currently. Nawrocki has approved more than 240 laws. He vetoed about 41. That's only 15 percent. But this is unique in the history of Poland. As much as Nawrocki blocked in just under a year, his predecessors didn't even turn down in two terms. A "peelful situation," says journalist Adam Szostkiewicz. The democratically elected President of the Republic of Poland destroyed the government's plans. After his election in the summer of 2025, he made it clear that he was taking the course of collision with the left-liberal coalition of Prime Minister Tusk. Tusk also made it clear early on that he would proceed "inconsiderately" if the president interfered with the government's business. Meanwhile, it has been shown how effectively Nawrocki can spread sand into the government gear. He blocked the laws of the government that wanted to regulate the crypto market three times. The trigger was a scandal with the largest Polish crypto exchange. Thousands of Polish women and Poles were unable to withdraw money. The situation made it clear that regulation is urgently needed. But to this day, there is no one - because of the three-time presidential veto, the cabinet has to submit a bill for the fourth time. Political opponents who would be obliged to cooperate: Poland's Prime Minister Tusk and President Nawrocki. The sympathizer of the Libertarian journalist Szostkiewicz finds the crypto-vetos of Nawrocki logical, after all the Polish president sympathizes with the US-Libertarians. This was entirely "in the spirit of the Trumpists": they wanted to prevent any regulation. "In return, you get the freedom to make as much money as possible - without any control," comments Szostkiewicz. The US is strategically important for Poland. The Tusk government reacted to the veto with a plan B - and found a way to get the money from Brussels past the president. But it got stuck: Nawrocki not only blocks, he also makes suggestions. Hard course towards Ukraine Even in the current history dispute with Ukraine, Nawrocki collected sympathy points. Since the end of May, there has been a conflict between the two countries. title: How the Riddle of a Holocaust Doer Was Solved
embedding_text:
Title: How the Riddle of a Holocaust Doer Was Solved

Sagittarius during mass shooting The solved riddle of a Holocaust doer The recording of an execution in the summer of 1941 is considered one of the most famous photos of the Holocaust. Much was unknown about the shooter for a long time - until now. How did James turn Onnen into a perpetrator? An execution 85 years ago: In July 1941, this scene took place in the German-occupied territories of the Soviet Union: an SS man targets the back of his victim with a gun. The photograph became an iconic image of the Holocaust, titled "The Last Jew of Winnytsia". The shooter was unknown until 2025, as was the exact location and timing of the recording. ==References====External links== The violence escalated in June 1941. Hundreds of thousands of people were murdered - men, women and children. The four emergency groups of the security police and the security service (SD) were significantly involved in this, which advanced behind the front line from north to south into the occupied territories of the Soviet Union. After a few weeks, Operation Group C reached the region around Shytomyr in the northwest of Ukraine. There, and not in Winnytsia, the iconic photo was taken: on the site of the Berdychiv citadel. That's what new research shows. The Mystery of Sagittarius Stefan Hördler is a historian at the University of Göttingen. His research focuses on the analysis of individuals and their networks involved in terror and murder during National Socialism. For the ARD podcast "NS-Cliquen: von Menschen und Murderern" he has chosen the biography of the perpetrator in the photo. In 2025 his identity had become known through the research of the family of the shooter and the historian Jürgen Matthäus from the USA: James Onnen, born 1906, from East Frisia. "There were many open questions about James Onnen's CV. Until 1939, one could decipher his career well, but then it became thin," explains Hördler. There was sometimes conflicting information that it had never been quite clear what his career looked like after the beginning of the Second World War and how he came to these shootings. "But we have solved this mystery now." Historian Hördler deals with people and their networks during National Socialism. The teacher and his desire for colonies James Onnen studied New Languages, English and French at the University of Göttingen from 1927. In 1932, he passed his first state examination and got a job at the Witzenhausen Colonial School. The school existed since 1898 and was meant to prepare young people for their service in the colonies. However, with the end of the First World War and the Versailles Treaty of 1919, Germany had lost all colonies. Onne considered this to be a condition that had to change again.

title: How the Riddle of a Holocaust Doer Was Solved
embedding_text:
Title: How the Riddle of a Holocaust Doer Was Solved

However, with the end of the First World War and the Versailles Treaty of 1919, Germany had lost all colonies. Onne considered this to be a condition that had to change again. In a publication called "raw materials from colonial space" he wrote in 1937: "Even now our living space is no longer enough to give work and bread to every fellow-people, and not without reason has the Führer so often pointed out the necessity of colonial possessions for Germany." For Hördler, a man with strong affinity for imperial ideas and the Nazi system is shown here. He also demonstrated his ideological closeness with memberships in various Nazi organizations. In 1931 he became a member of the NSDAP, and in the following year he became a member of the SA. In 1933 he moved to the SS. The questions of the relatives Parallel to months of archival research, Hördler also visited the family of James Onnen in Bremen. Johanna Hellmerichs is the niece of James Onnen. Her husband, Klaus Hellmerichs, has been researching intensively for years to shed light on the career of Onnen. Together they went through all existing documents. But the open questions remained. A career in NSDAP or SS has never done Onnen, taught instead. But how did he become one of the groups? The known members were mostly men of the security police and the SD. But this did not include Onnen. What is hardly known to the public is that 20 to 30 percent of the men in each of the operational groups were also members of the police and reserve forces of the Waffen-SS. "I have looked at hundreds of other men who have undergone similar recruitment patterns, who have had a similar age and who have alternated between missions in the SS deadhead species and the civilian profession," says Hördler. "And then I was able to prove that these men - including numerous teachers - were systematically recruited to the deployment groups as reserve troops of the Waffen-SS. Men like James Onnen." From school to the shootings Shortly before the beginning of the war he came directly in connection with terror and violence: Onnnen became the 2. SS-Totenkopfstandarte "Brandenburg" (Concentration Camp Sachsenhausen), then in 1940 to the reinforced SS-Totenkopfstandarte in the German-occupied areas of Poland, where he was temporarily also working as training director of the police. After a short return to the school service, the decisive convocation took place in mid-January 1941: as a reservist of the Waffen-SS directly into the installation room of the Einsatzgruppen around Bad Schmiedeberg. Onnen was formally part of other units, but instead was part of the C unit, which was located in Berdychiv in July 1941. The photo of the crime in the diary In a diary of a Wehrmacht officer, which became known in 2021, the photo of the shooting was found, labeled with the citadel Berdychiv as a crime scene and dated to the 28th century. ==References== More than 70 people were killed that day. The information is in line with the events reported by Einsatzgruppe C at the time, as historian Hördler explains. It seems that spectators were not only present at this execution. ==References== In July 1941, the Reichssicherheitshauptamt issued the order that the gathering of spectators during mass executions should be prevented. The historian stresses that participation in shootings was not mandatory. It was certainly desired by the managers that in the sense of a group connection and also a certain distribution of guilt everyone co-joined. But in the research of thousands of biographies, Hördler also found men who refused to be transferred or even if all the violence became ill and withdrew from the ministry. This did not lead to a disadvantage for the people. More on the topic in the third season of the ARD podcast "NS-Cliquen: von Menschen und Murderern".

title: Why concert tickets are getting more expensive
embedding_text:
Title: Why concert tickets are getting more expensive

Trend at staggered prices Why concert tickets are getting more expensive If you want to go to a concert in the USA, you have to pay some thousands of dollars. And in this country, too, it is becoming more and more expensive, while small festivals are fighting for survival. How do prices even arise? The tour of US superstar Taylor Swift two years ago has set new standards. Since then, many fans have been willing to spend several thousand dollars to be close to their superstar for an evening. The ticket prices for the stadium tours of German artists such as Helene Fischer have also increased significantly. Nicolas Ruth, expert for digital communication in the music and entertainment industry at the University of Würzburg, explains the economy behind the ticket prices: "We have a trend at staggered prices. For example, VIP tickets that are particularly expensive." Concerts by thirty percent more expensive than before the pandemic In fact, concerts today are thirty percent more expensive than before the Corona pandemic, which was calculated by the Bundesverband der Konzert- und Veranstaltungswirtschaft (Bundesverband der Konzert- und Veranstaltungswirtschaft). At the same time, musicians increasingly rely on the live business. Because while streaming brings reach, the money is mainly earned on tour, according to entertainment expert Ruth. Many concerts are sold out and festivals are well in demand. There it was necessary to catch the transport costs and the partly complex technology that is needed by the ticket prices. Increasing production costs are also a topic in the concert industry, says the expert. But the increased production costs alone do not explain the development of ticket prices. Dynamic ticket prices Organizers increasingly rely on so-called dynamic pricing. Means: The price depends on the demand. If you want to buy tickets that are particularly popular, you often pay significantly more than originally announced, says Ruth. "An algorithm determines on a website how the offer is for a price. We know this from other areas, such as tourism or e-commerce." This is partly intransparent and unfair, according to the digital expert vis-à-vis the ARD financial editor. From the point of view of the organisers, however, it is understandable that the customers' willingness to pay is to be depleted before this happens on a second market, says Ruth. The market is dominated by only a few major suppliers.The concert market is characterised by increasingly fewer and smaller companies, where smaller companies are almost unable to compete. In Germany there is a market concentration among the exchange-traded organisers Livenation and CTS Eventim. For fans, this can often mean higher fees and less transparency. Nevertheless, many people are willing to spend more and more money on a concert evening. Thomas Renz is a research assistant at the Institute for Cultural Participation Research in Berlin, and he observes a change from possession to experience. The concert was more than just entertainment. "It signals belonging to a particular scene, to a temporary community of values." Especially international megastars create events in which many have the feeling: Here I meet like-minded people, says the participation researcher. Small festivals under pressure However, the boom of the big tours also has a downside. While international stars easily fill stadiums, smaller festivals and medium-sized acts are increasingly under economic pressure. Because their costs rise the same way - they can not increase the ticket prices in any way.

title: Three findings on black and red for the summer break

Title: Three findings on black and red for the summer break

Government coalition How black and red starts the summer break After a difficult year, black and red is now in a struggle for reform. The pressure to deliver is high. It remains open whether Union and SPD can regain lost trust. Three findings on the government coalition. "The coalition has taken step," Friedrich Merz summed it up in his summer press conference. Attesting this is quite late - after over a year of government. But it describes the initially bumpy development of this purpose-community quite correctly: the two very different coalition partners had to learn the cohesion first. Union leader Jens Spahn, in particular, had to learn to involve centrifugal forces at his own political margins in order to grant stable majorities - for example, the young group of the Union faction, which pretty much stirred up the first pension compromise last autumn. 1. Reform-Eile has its price The pressure to deliver rose steadily. Some due to poor survey values, but he was also home-made: The words "We have achieved a lot, but not enough" have become the Chancellor's permanent mantra, which looks above all at the weakening economy. The pension reform was overworked: three younger deputies from the rebels were appointed to the pension commission with experts. These met in long public noiseless internal meetings so that they could present a 33-point reform plan, which had already been agreed for the Union and the SPD, together with the Commission in June. The health insurance savings package had come by the Bundestag and the Bundesrat shortly before the parliamentary summer break - which was not considered secure for a long time. But the rush has its price: shortly before the vote, there had been major changes - from the opposition's point of view, there was no time for a careful examination. Individual measures also pose the question of how precisely they have been considered on their consequences, such as cuts in the field of psychotherapy. This has now become clear to the Federal Government itself, and by means of a motion for a resolution they want to take up the issue again after the summer break. The reform could have been passed even after the summer break - but the coalition of the trumps to be ready was apparently more important. Destroying earthquakes Number of deaths in Venezuela increases to more than 5,000 More than three weeks after the double earthquake in Venezuela, the number of victims has exceeded 5,000. title: Dead and injured after landslide in western China
embedding_text:
Title: Dead and injured after landslide in western China

In Western China, dead and injured after landslide In a landslide, at least eight people have been killed in China. More than ten houses have been spilled, dozens of people are still missing. The disaster occurred east of the million-dollar city of Chongqing. At least eight people have died in a landslide in western China. 34 other people were still missing, the authorities informed on late Friday evening (local time). The landslide occurred in the Pengshui region, east of the million-city Chongqing. According to CCTV, more than 800 rescue workers were sent to the scene of the disaster. Referring to the authorities, the state media also said that more than ten homes were buried and people were trapped. Ten buried people have thus been saved and are out of danger of life. Shortly before the demolition, local residents had noticed rockfalls, state television reported. During the subsequent evacuation of 60 inhabitants, the rubble avalanche disappeared, it was said. Photographs published by CCTV showed a large accumulation of rock and earth covering part of a road at the foot of a mountain in the region. In addition, there were people who screamed and fled from a cloud of dust. Second landslide within one month According to the government, around 6.45 million euros of aid were provided to support the rescue and relief measures and to provide assistance to the affected residents. President Xi Jinping asked the authorities to quickly investigate the cause of the accident. It was only at the beginning of the month that a landslide occurred in the northwestern Chinese province of Gansu, killing 21 people.
