After Ministerial Dismissal Renewed protests in Kiev In Ukraine there have been again demonstrations against the dismissal of the popular defense minister Fedorov. Thousands of protesters took to the streets in Kiev. On Friday evening, thousands of people protested against the replacement of the popular defense minister Mychailo Fedorov in the Ukrainian capital Kiev. The demonstrators waved Ukrainian flags and held up posters on which "Bring Fedorov back!" and "Respect the people!". For many Fedorov stood for modernization Fedorov had resigned on Wednesday in the context of a government reshaping of President Wolodymyr Selenskyj. The head of state had established the remodelling of the cabinet with "new challenges and tasks" and had also dismissed Prime Minister Julia Swyrydenko. Especially the replacement of Fedorov met with opposition from the citizens. For many Ukrainians, the minister-in-office for only six months embodied the modernisation of the army burdened by bureaucracy and corruption accusations. Fedorov had driven the digitalisation of the armed forces and the deployment of drones. Under his direction, the salaries of soldiers were significantly increased and plans for a gradual demobilization were developed. On Thursday, Selenskyy had appointed the intelligence officer Yevheny Chmara as the managing minister of defence. Parliament still has to agree with the staff. Already on Thursday in Kiev, many people had taken to the streets for Fedorov's stay in office, and there were protests in other cities as well.

title: USA and Iran: Illegal war - but permissible sea blockade?
embedding_text:
Title: USA and Iran: Illegal war - but permissible sea blockade?

USA and Iran Illegal war - but permissible sea blockade? To put Iran under pressure, the US is blocking the ports in the Strait of Hormus. A tanker who tried to break through the blockade was shot at. A toll was also in discussion. Is all this covered by international law? Can the U.S. military fire ships to enforce a U.S. blockade of Iranian ports in the Strait of Hormus? The answer is given by the customary law of nations. Its rules are not found in written laws or international treaties. They arise because they are used over a long period of time - and other states accept that. The unwritten rules also play a role in the street of Hormus. And bind both Iran and the USA. Sea blockades are allowed during the war "The right to transit through straits that are important for international shipping is also part of the customary international law," says Wolff Heintschel von Heinegg. The lawyer is an emeritus professor of international law and researches maritime war law. The Strait of Hormus is such a strait with international significance. Under the right to transit, ships must be able to travel through the strait. They must also not be affected by a blockade. This is true at least in times of peace. It's different in times of war. Then the law of the sea applies. "The US could claim that blockades in the maritime war are not excluded as a means of warfare," says Pierre Thielbörger, professor of international law at the Ruhr University Bochum. Restrictions apply. The blockade must be announced in advance, and only part of the lake must be affected. "Even in the maritime war, one must not block an entire strait, but must set the blockade against the ports of the opposing conflict party," says Thielbörger. "The US claims that these conditions are met, but it is difficult to judge from a distance. However, it seems to be rather remote to speak of a blockade of the entire road of Hormus." International law: Fire on ships permitted by the USA The assessment also shares Heintschel von Heinegg. The blockade of Iranian ports by the US military was allowed. Technically, the blockade was a line that should not be crossed by ships. The state that pulls the blockade - the US - may also defend it. "This must be effective and impartial," says Heintschel von Heinegg. The U.S. military must enforce the blockade against any ship trying to break through the blockade. Forbidden is to let through only ships of friendly states, while others are not, according to the international law. Therefore, the recent US action in the Strait of Hormus is also allowed. The United States reported last Wednesday that it had fired a tanker. According to the U.S. military regional command, CENTCOM, the ship was on its way to an Iranian port blocked by the U.S. military.

title: USA and Iran: Illegal war - but permissible sea blockade?
embedding_text:
Title: USA and Iran: Illegal war - but permissible sea blockade?

Therefore, the recent US action in the Strait of Hormus is also allowed. The United States reported last Wednesday that it had fired a tanker. According to the U.S. military regional command, CENTCOM, the ship was on its way to an Iranian port blocked by the U.S. military. According to CENTCOM, the tanker ignored US military warnings and tried to break the blockade. As a result, he had been shot at and disembarked. Rules also apply in war against international law What role does it play, however, that most international law students assume that the US war against Iran as a whole is contrary to international law? Can a blockade of Iranian ports be permitted, even though the war is not? International Law Professor Pierre Thielbörger explains that it is always necessary to distinguish between the use of military force and how it may be used. Even in a war that was initiated in violation of international law, a blockade of individual ports under maritime war law may be permitted. The right to war and the right to war are two different things. The right to war determines when a state may attack another state because it defends itself or is legitimized by the UN Security Council. The right in war also regulates what action is permitted in a war. It is intended to protect the people who are at war, i.e. the soldiers and the civilian population. Her suffering is always the same. They must therefore be protected in a war permitted under international law as well as in a war contrary to international law. According to international law expert Thielbörger, "if a concrete blockade against ports may be permitted under maritime war law in individual cases, this does of course not change the fact that the use of military force may be illegal in general."Special regulations for sea mines However, a blockade of the entire Strait of Hormus does not allow international law, Thielbörger stresses: "Even under maritime war law, Iran cannot claim to be allowed to block the Strait of Hormus - here no reference to the ports of another conflict party that would be effectively blocked." Special rules apply under maritime war law for the use of sea mines. They must not interfere with neutral shipping. The state that uses them must also ensure that it retains control of the mines at all times. Straits such as the Strait of Hormus may only be mined if the state proposes safe and acceptable alternative routes. "Iran has violated these rules in several ways," says Thielbörger. There are indications that Iran does not have sufficient control over the mines. In addition, the road from Hormus lacks necessary alternative routes. International law prohibits a toll The right to transit does not only give ships a right to travel through the strait without shelling. It also prohibits states from charging for transit. Therefore, a toll for the Strait of Hormus would also violate international law. Since the beginning of the war, such a person has been in constant discussion. It was only at the beginning of this week that US President Donald Trump announced that the US would charge a fee for the passage. In return, the U.S. military ships should allow safe passage. One day later, the US president withdrew from his plan. Iran had also announced a toll in April of this year. Wartime can change people's custom law Even if a toll is currently in violation of people's custom law, this could change in future. For the law of the people continues to develop. Events such as those in the street of Hormus play a major role in this. "On the one hand, it is interesting to see how Iran and the US behave in the current events in the Strait of Hormus, but on the other hand, how other states react," says Thielbörger. This applies "especially in the case of rather unusual operations, such as the collection of a toll for a passage". If the United States were to charge a toll and other states would accept it in the long term, this could help to allow for such a toll in the future. Under current law, however, they are clearly prohibited.

title: US and Iran report new mutual attacks in the Gulf region
embedding_text:
Title: US and Iran report new mutual attacks in the Gulf region

Conflict in the Gulf region US and Iran continue mutual attacks The dispute over the Strait of Hormus between the US and Iran seems to be getting more and more out of control: The seventh night in a row there were mutual attacks - and Iran threatens to escalate even further. The U.S. military claimed to have attacked targets in Iran for the seventh consecutive night. According to their own statements, the armed forces bombed Iranian surveillance facilities, underground weapons camps, and other military infrastructure. The aim was to further weaken Iran's military capabilities, the U.S. military (CENTCOM) command in charge of the region said. Iranian media, however, again spoke of attacks on civilian infrastructure. Iranian state television reported explosions in the southern Iranian city of Sirik and the coastal city of Bandar Abbas. The attacks hit bridges, an airport and maritime infrastructure, they said. Iran's UN ambassador then declared that the US attacks on civilian infrastructure constituted war crimes. In the coastal region of Jask, the supply of drinking water for at least 10,000 people was reportedly interrupted by the state. The background is the bombing of a seawater desalination plant, reported the government's press service on the Hormusgan province's waterworks. US President Donald Trump had recently threatened to attack Iran's infrastructure if the leadership in Tehran should not take a turn in the conflict. Attacks on U.S. bases As in the previous nights, Iran has resorted to retaliation. The Gulf States of Kuwait and Bahrain were again under attack. Explosions were also reported from Saudi Arabia and Jordan. According to Iran's state media, at least two US fighters and three other aircraft were destroyed in an attack in Jordan. In Kuwait, according to the government, another plant for power generation and seawater desalination was hit by an Iranian attack. A fire broke out. According to a report from the Wall Street Journal, the United States moved more fighter jets from Europe to the Middle East. According to information from the US news portal Axios, the US government informed the ally of Israel that it would send dozens of more tankers to the country ahead of a possible expansion of military operations against Iran. Mohsen Rezaei, advisor to Iran's Supreme Leader, warned that his country would enter a "phase of comprehensive offensive and destruction operations" if the US attacks lasted two or three days. Iran would no longer limit itself to retaliation, the Arab channel Al Jazeera quoted him. Iran reports explosions on oil tankers According to the Iranian Revolutionary Guards, two oil tankers have also been hit by mines and "exploded" in the Strait of Hormus. At first, the Revolutionary Guard did not provide information on the nationality of the ships or possible victims. The U.S. regional commando CENTCOM contradicted the presentation. According to the Iranian authorities, the recent US airstrikes cost at least 46 lives. More than 400 had been injured. US representatives confirmed that 13 more US soldiers had been injured since Monday, but did not give any details. An end to the Iran war is not currently in sight. US President Donald Trump had threatened to increase pressure on Iran because it impedes shipping in the Strait of Hormus. The Hormus road was actually to be reopened for international shipping in the course of a framework agreement signed a month ago. However, after the mutual attacks that have now resumed, shipping traffic in the strait has almost come to a halt.

title: Union leader Spahn defends surrogacy against criticism

Title: Union leader Spahn defends surrogacy against criticism

Spahn defends surrogacy "I have been torn apart for a long time" The demands for a resignation of Spahn become louder. Now the CDU politician has defended the decision for surrogacy: it was "no easy decisions". The group should decide on its future. Union leader Jens Spahn justified his decision to have a child via a surrogate mother. In a podcast with picture journalist Paul Ronzheimer, he explained that he himself had long "tormented about the subject of surrogacy". "I've been torn apart for a long time," Spahn said. "But just about this struggle and dealing with the subject, we have chosen this path." He knows it "as a Christian" that "one is the pure teaching and the other is the real life". It was "no easy decisions," he said. CDU against legalization Spahn and his husband Daniel Funke announced on Wednesday that they have become parents by a surrogate mother in the USA. The case triggered a controversy because surrogacy is not permitted in Germany. Spahn's own party also clearly opposes legalization - just as Spahn himself did in the past. He is now also confronted with demands for resignation from his own party. Federal Chancellor Friedrich Merz had informed him about paternity the week before last. He congratulated him, Spahn said. But both would also have talked about "that this is not an easy starting point". "I will, of course, discuss the question of how to proceed with the group when we meet again in September," said the CDU politician. Commenting on requests for resignation, Spahn said: "In the end, only the group can decide on how to proceed." The first regular group meeting after the parliamentary summer break will take place on 8 October. ==References== Merz emphasizes clear legal situation Chancellor Merz stressed on Friday that it should also remain with the existing ban in Germany. "We have a clear legal situation in Germany. We have a party resolution in the CDU. I do not see that changes should be made to the legal situation or to the decision on the party congress," he said in the margins of Franco-German talks in Brühl. Everything else will be discussed at the next meeting of the CDU Bureau.

title: Franco-German Summit: More cooperation in armaments
embedding_text:
Title: Franco-German Summit: More cooperation in armaments

Franco-German summit More cooperation - especially in defence The joint fighter jet failed, but Germany and France want to expand their military cooperation. This was announced by Chancellor Merz and President Macron after the Franco-German Council of Ministers. In focus: atomic deterrence. Federal Chancellor Friedrich Merz and French President Emmanuel Macron demonstrate against a historic backdrop in Brühl, North Rhine-Westphalia, after the 26th century. German-French Council of Ministers the unity of their two countries. At Augustusburg Castle, where in 1962 the then French President Charles de Gaulle had offered German Chancellor Konrad Adenauer a Franco-German friendship agreement, which later became the Elysée Treaty, Merz and Macron agreed an even closer cooperation. Bundeswehr participates in nuclear exercise This is particularly about defence. "We are taking a new common path in nuclear deterrence," Merz said at the joint press conference. This includes "conventional German forces taking part in a nuclear exercise by the French armed forces this year". The first German military involvement in the French army's nuclear exercise was "complementary to our nuclear participation and the deterrence in NATO that we hold on to," the Chancellor continued. The relocation of French Rafale fighter jets to Nörvenich in NRW is also a "first operational step in strategic cooperation", says the Council's final statement. Macron: No German funding Macron said that future cooperation in nuclear deterrence did not aim at Berlin participating in the financing of French nuclear weapons. France has always financed its nuclear armament itself and will continue to do so. "What is the goal? It is about improving the security of the European continent and creating greater uncertainty among our opponents by opening up further ways and means to nuclear deterrence," the French President said. Further work on FCAS components Despite the partial failure of the FCAS fighter aircraft, parts of the joint defence project are to be continued. Merz and Macron stated that Germany and France would continue to develop a so-called Combat Cloud for the networking of weapons systems. "FCAS was never just a new fighter aircraft, but was always a system," Merz said. "And we stick to this system, we continue to expand it." In the government consultations, this technological cooperation was put on a new foundation. Germany and France also want to deepen their cooperation in key technologies such as artificial intelligence, space, nuclear fusion and quantum technology. "The German hand always remains outstretched" Both Merz and Macron emphasized the relevance of the Franco-German friendship. According to Macron, the agreement between the two countries is a prerequisite for Europe to move forward. Merz spoke of challenging times when the connection between Germany and France was indispensable. Regardless of the outcome of the presidential election in France next year, the Chancellor continued to ensure close cooperation with the neighbouring country. "From a German point of view, I can only say that we will continue to do everything we can to make this cooperation with our French neighbour as intense and as deep and trusting as possible, regardless of how the voters in France will decide," says Merz. He assumed that every successor would be based on exactly what was right and what was necessary in the interests of both countries, Merz added. "The German hand always remains outstretched to deepen and trustful cooperation with France." For a long time, polls have seen Marine Le Pen ahead, at least in the first round of the French presidential elections.

title: Israel's Parliament Dissolves - Campaign Begins

Up to the last minute, his right-wing coalition parties whipped numerous controversial laws through parliament. Thus, the Knesset passed a protection law to prevent ultra-orthodox conscientious objectors from being arrested. Compulsory military service Central campaign topic Opposition leader Yair Lapid reacted with horror: "So you end the legislature by spitting the Israeli soldier in the face. My two sons were drafted into the army and took up their duties. You didn't come up with the idea that someone wouldn't do that. Our party says: conscientious objectors do not get a shekel from the state." The compulsory military service for ultraorthodoxes has become the central topic in the election campaign. While young men who are drafted will have to serve for 32 months in the future, Torah students refuse to serve, even though soldiers are urgently needed in the war. Israel's Supreme Judges froze the law to protect deserters from arrest. Arrests of deserters are still allowed. With the advance, Netanyahu wants to secure the support of his ultra-orthodox coalition partners. However, this sends a devastating signal to the population, says Adam Shinar, professor of constitutional law at Reichman University. "It shows where the priorities of this government lie. This government is not popular in Israel, but successful in achieving its own party goals. It's a disaster for the country. The division between Israel's secular and religious society is greater than four years ago." A law that could restrict the freedom of the press in Israel by giving the government more power over media funding had also been passed at the last moment. Policy experts such as Shinar warn of a general attack on Israel's democracy. Weakening of the Attorney General So another law weakens the competences of the Attorney General of Israel. As chief prosecutor, she directs state prosecution against, among other things, top politicians. Dina Zilber was also a state attorney in Israel until 2020. In the ARD, she said that the new law would be the binding opinion of the Attorney General on the recommendation that the government could ignore. That's how the government interprets the laws. Governments could take a private lawyer and evade the opinion of the Attorney General's office, says Zilber. "Only the Supreme Court can lift illegal attacks by a government. But many government projects do not reach the court at all. This new law endangers Israeli democracy." October Now there could be protests in the population again, observers predict. The election campaign in Israel is now entering the hot phase, which will last until 27 July. ==References== Then a new parliament will be elected in Israel.

title: New Labour boss Andy Burnham: his recipe is regionalization

Title: New Labour boss Andy Burnham: his recipe is regionalization

Future British Prime Minister Burnham His recipe is regionalization More money and responsibility for the regions: The future British Prime Minister Burnham does not want to govern only in and for London - and thus boost the economy. Expectations are high, but also the challenges. Andy Burnham has repeatedly presented an idea over the past few weeks: it is his vision of how he wants to bring Britain forward, how he wants to generate growth. The regions should be strengthened, more power for local administrations, especially in the agglomerations. Burnham stated that he would move parts of the administration to Manchester on Downing Street: A Downing Street No. 10 in the north - that's never happened before. He himself would not be there exclusively, but he would be there again and again. He wanted to promote growth in all regions. "In Manchester, I proved how we can create growth," he told LBC. That's Andy Burnham's story. Less London-centered The politician from the north, who also has a slight north-English accent, wants to strengthen the regions. Self-responsibility for local politicians, more budget rights, own tax revenues, that's what the plan looks like. This sounds unusual for German ears because federalism is strong in the Federal Republic and tax revenues are also drained at the municipal level. This is different in the United Kingdom. Britain is extremely centralized. If there is a major investment in the region, the politicians in London will have to promote funding. It is remarkable that Burnham is gaining listeners with such a sober vision, which has more to do with the theory of state building. Maybe because it sounds so normal, it can also be self-critical. His generation of politicians had failed in many areas, he formulated in an interview. The country is not where it should be. The country had to be set up. That's exactly what many people feel. Burnham promises a lot - the expectations for him are high. Starmer's faster crash than warning Keir Starmer had to experience how fast the mood in the country can turn. In 2024, Starmer won a brilliant victory for Labour - two years later he was swept away by a lost regional and local election. People had also expected a lot from Starmer, but reforms - and this is likely to be Burnham's challenge - need time. Burnham is perceived as being close, the nice neighbor next door. Very different from Keir Starmer, who was always the lawyer and was considered to be "posh", as noble, from good home, although his father was a craftsman. Interestingly, Burnham has put on a career that could be overwritten with "establishment". After studying, he started in a representative's office in Westminster, at 31, he himself moved into parliament as a representative of the people, and later even became a minister. Achievements in Manchester make him credible Since 2017, he has been mayor of Manchester - after unsuccessfully running for the party president. So this time it worked out, party chairman he is already once, on Monday he should be elected prime minister. His experience in Manchester and the history of regional development, which he embodies so credibly, are likely to have contributed significantly to this. Maurice Lange, analyst at the Centre for Cities think tank, says: "This is a political message in the sense that people outside of London feel separated from the decisions taken in London." More decisions in the region - many of them could gather behind it. Maurice Lange believes that further federalization - a outsourcing of power to the regions - is essential for growth. And growth is the most important thing Britain needs now.

title: Shared reactions to EU proposal on emissions trading
embedding_text:
Title: Shared reactions to EU proposal on emissions trading

EU proposal on emissions trading A good day for the climate or a disappointment? With a reform of emissions trading, the EU Commission aims to relieve the burden on industry. For this purpose, the revenues are to flow more specifically into decarbonisation. The reactions are split. The fact that emissions trading (ETS) is at the heart of EU climate policy leaves no doubt about it: "No other political instrument has reduced emissions to a similar extent," he enthuses and speaks of "a phenomenal tool in our climate policy toolbox". Nevertheless, the Commission wants to take this. On the one hand, because domestic companies have increasingly come under pressure from the global competition promoted by state subsidies. But the European companies themselves are doing too little, Hoekstra criticises. And then there are the member states that spend too little money from the ETS on reducing CO2 emissions. EU criticises the use of ETS revenues "Member States receive around 80 percent of ETS profits. But just ten percent of this really goes into decarbonization. That was always the promise," explains Hoekstra. The ETS should not be a tax, but an engine for the decarbonisation of industry. Now the Commission wants to meet industry, but also to put on the thumbscrews. For a time, there will be more allowances for emissions trading than originally planned. This takes some pressure from companies to reduce their carbon dioxide emissions: it extends the period and reduces costs. The amount of allowances available is constantly decreasing so that they become more expensive and climate investment pays more. Particularly energy-intensive industries - including the chemical and steel industries - are already receiving some of their certificates free of charge. Climate neutrality remains the target for 2050 But the money that comes in through the ETS should go more specifically and fully into climate action in Europe. In addition, the Commission is extending the ETS system, for example to air and maritime transport. "Today's proposals are fully in line, and I would like to stress this again, fully in line with our climate targets for 2040," says Climate Commissioner Hoekstra. And that means that, as measured by the 1990 level, 90 per cent of greenhouse gases are to be emitted by 2040, and by 2050 climate neutrality is to be achieved. Praise from Christian Democrats, SPD criticism This is a good day for the climate, says Peter Liese of the Christian Democrats in the European Parliament: "Anyone who wanted to stop or completely abolish the ETS must now see that this will not happen. The ETS will remain, and it will bring benefits to those who invest in climate neutrality." Timo Wölken, S & D spokesperson on environmental policy, is disappointed, however, that "only 85 percent of CO2 emissions are saved in the ETS sectors, even if we have just established by law that 90 percent must be saved." EU members and parliament still have to agree To compensate for international climate credits and possible CO2 withdrawals from the atmosphere, Wölken does not find it safe enough. Nor is the obligation to invest in Europe sufficient. "Social conditionality, which means that jobs will also be preserved, is clearly shortening our social democrats," Wölken continues and concludes: "This package is a total disappointment and needs to be improved urgently." The opportunity will come, because the Commission's proposal still needs the approval of the Member States and the European Parliament.

title: Russia: War enemy Nadezhdin sentenced to fine
embedding_text:
Title: Russia: War enemy Nadezhdin sentenced to fine

Opposition in Russia Court Condemns War Opponents Nadezhdin to Penalty A Russian court has sentenced opposition politician Nadezhdin to a fine. The declared war opponent sees political motives in the action of the authorities - in connection with the upcoming election. The opposition politician and war opponent Boris Nadezhdin has been sentenced to a fine in Russia for the public display of "extremist symbols". The court in Dolgoprudny near Moscow imposed a fine of 1,000 rubles (about eleven euros) on him. Nadezhdin had been arrested at the beginning of the week. Nadezhdin's lawyers had previously requested a postponement of the sentence in vain after paramedics had examined the 63-year-old for high blood pressure in the courtroom. After the verdict, Nadezhdin said, "I am glad to be alive." Russia is a "sick state". "Nervous" for parliamentary elections? The accusations against Nadezhdin related to a contribution in social media. It also featured a picture of opposition politician Alexei Navalny, who died in prison in 2024. Navalny and his organizations are considered "extremist" in Russia. An offense, as charged to Nadezhdin, can result in a sentence of up to 15 days' imprisonment. But even the comparatively low fine has far-reaching consequences: if the verdict becomes final, as is to be expected, Nadezhdin must not participate in the parliamentary election in September. Nadezhdin had rejected the allegations. The authorities went against him because they were "nervous" because of his "popularity" and a possible candidacy of a war opponent for parliament, he said. Demand for the end of the war in Ukraine Nadezhdin actually wanted to take part in the parliamentary election in September in his home constituency of Mytishchi. He was a member of the Russian Parliament from 1999 to 2003. In the presidential election two years ago, he wanted to run against Vladimir Putin. But his candidacy was ultimately not allowed by the Electoral Commission. Nadezhdin calls for an end to the Russian war against Ukraine and hopes for a "peaceful change of power" in Russia. He is considered one of the last Kremlin and war critics who openly express criticism and are neither in exile nor in prison. With information from Philip Raillon, ARD-Studio Moscow

There, the EU Member States' specialised ministers discuss and decide on the policy of the European Union. Depending on the subject, for example, the Finance, Agriculture or Economic Ministers meet there. Most recently, the Council of Ministers negotiated common asylum rules and sanctions against Russia. Dissatisfaction with the traffic light government Under Olaf Scholz as Chancellor, the Federal Government in the Council of Ministers had repeatedly noticed negatively with the so-called "German Vote". Germany abstained on important decisions because the traffic light government did not agree. From Friedrich Merz, the European partners hoped for a different picture and, above all, more leadership. After all, a former Member of the EU Parliament came back to Brussels with him. He and his federal ministers are now in office for more than a year. An evaluation of the ARD studio Brussels shows that the current ministers of the Federal Government do not meet their colleagues at the Councils of Ministers more often than their predecessors. The attendance rate of the Merz government is even lower than at traffic lights. title: Absent in Brussels: How often German ministers are missing at EU meetings
embedding_text:
Title: Absent in Brussels: How often German ministers are missing at EU meetings

Strack-Zimmermann sees "air up" This is a matter of prioritisation, says Marie-Agnes Strack-Zimmermann of the FDP, who used to sit in the Bundestag and is now a member of the European Parliament: "That ministers say that they have to see at home that everything is going on. That's the one. But the other is, what kind of access do you have to Europe? I believe that there is still a lot of air up there." At the request of the ARD-Studio Brussels, a speaker from the Federal Government says that the decline in attendance is: "Of course, the Chancellor's claim is to play a decisive role in European political dossiers; accordingly, in addition to the specialist ministers, the Federal Chancellor is also campaigning for German positions in Brussels." State Secretarys at Councils of Ministers The evaluation shows, however, that the German ministers are always represented by their secretaries of state, i.e. by the highest-ranking officials in the ministry. This is not a problem in principle, says Andreas Maurer, professor of political science with a focus on European integration at the University of Innsbruck. It is important that the Federal Republic of Germany is represented in a decision-making capacity and that the person sitting there can speak and vote. "State Secretarys can do that at least as well as ministers," says Maurer. After all, undersecretaries of state have often worked on a dossier longer than an elected minister. The political impact of absence The technical aspect is one, but it is also about the political impact, says Jannis Emmanuelilidis of the European Policy Center: "The signal is sent that the topic or the general discussion of solutions at European level is not so highly valued." He has observed the tendency that ministers are less likely to be among the Councils of Ministers for a long time. Marie-Agnes Strack-Zimmermann says that if German ministers are missing from the negotiating table, this would be noticeable: "Of course all of Europe is looking at Germany." Dutch ministers are more frequent There are EU Member States that send their ministers to the councils more often than Germany, for example the Netherlands. But there are also countries whose ministers are less likely to arrive. This includes Poland. That was the case at the time of the traffic lights and that has not changed under Friedrich Merz as Chancellor. But Merz had announced this differently. Andreas Maurer of the University of Innsbruck assumes that today's Federal Chancellor has misunderstood himself: "It is said to Mr Merz that he is good at inflating the cheeks thickly and now and then communicating what is not quite reflected." At the same time, many observe that Friedrich Merz is more active in the EU than his predecessor Olaf Scholz was. "It shows a stronger commitment at European level and shows that it has understood the importance of finding common solutions at European level," says Jannis Emmanuelidis of the European Policy Center. In the end, there is a discrepancy between the appearance of the Chancellor in Brussels and that of his ministers.

title: Number of apprenticeship contracts in the craft sector increased
embedding_text:
Title: Number of apprenticeship contracts in the craft sector increased

Central Association of German Crafts Training figures in the craft sector increased The craft sector in Germany again finds more young people - the number of training contracts has increased. Craft President Dittrich speaks of a glance at the light and mentions several reasons for the rise. In the craft sector, training numbers are rising against the trend in the economy as a whole: Jörg Dittrich, President of the Central Association of German Craftsmen, spoke of a bright light in an overall difficult situation. "We are heading towards the fourth year in a row with rising new contract numbers," said Dittrich of the news agency dpa. Between January and June 2026, almost 67,800 new training contracts were already registered in the apprentice roles - more than 3,160 contracts and 4.9 percent more than in the same period last year. "We last had such a high June value in 2018. This is a strong message: young people consciously decide for the craft." In some chambers there are even double-digit increases. Several reasons for increasing training figures Dittrich mentioned several reasons for increasing training numbers in the craft sector: "First: AI. Young people realize that the craft sector offers a real alternative if they want a safe job." Secondly, the image campaign of the craft sector and advertising efforts have obviously arrived in society. Thirdly, the opportunities for earning and career in the craft sector would have improved. "And fourthly, parts of the industry have massively reduced the number of their training places due to the global market situation." Dittrich demanded that the responsibility of the craft industry in the training should be appreciated accordingly. The equivalence of academic and vocational education and training should finally be enshrined in law. "And it must be made clear in terms of financial resources." The education centres would need considerable renovation.

title: According to the study, what is going on against racism in authorities?
embedding_text:
Title: According to the study, what is going on against racism in authorities?

According to an extensive study What is doing against racism in authorities? A study initiated by the Ministry of the Interior in February showed that racism is a structural problem in authorities. Only the publication was conspicuously reserved. What follows the results now? When Mary came to Germany from Mexico many years ago, she had a vision: she wanted to learn the language, study it again and realize herself professionally. She didn't know how hard it would be. Mary actually means different and does not want to talk about her experiences publicly. For example, about the fact that their training in Germany has never been recognised by an authority. Or that once when she was looking for a job, she was told: Yes, there is something - "just for people like you". It was meant for the unskilled. Time and again she felt that what she had learned in her home country was not good enough. And that this could have to do with her origin - even though she is now a German citizen. "No matter how much I know, how much I can, it's not recognized here," she says. Study: Racism in Authorities Structured The fact that it is not alone with these experiences is shown by the study "Institutions and Racism" (InRa) published in February: In authorities, the habit of seeing certain "migrants first as uneducated, unmotivated [...] and as 'different' is dominating," it says. This condescending approach would be applied to those affected by their educational biographies. A central result of the study is that racism is structured in German institutions. It occurs in all the authorities under investigation - in job centres, immigration authorities, police, customs, justice, health and youth services. According to an employee survey, public authorities do not have a uniformly increased level of ethnically discriminatory attitudes compared to the population. Racism often does not appear obvious, but hides itself: through stereotypes, microaggression, disrespectful language, or unequal treatment of decisions and achievements. In order to counteract this, the authors developed concrete proposals for action. In addition, the study advocates greater diversity in administration, discriminatory recruitment and promotion procedures, and greater scientific monitoring and evaluation of the measures. Publication extremely reserved At the Hessisches Landkreistag for example, however, the study was not known until the request by the HR. It's not surprising. Because the responsible Federal Ministry of the Interior under Alexander Dobrindt (CSU) presented the results very cautiously. Instead of a press conference or a message to the media, there was only one message on the website - in the section "Short News". Project manager Gert Pickel is disappointed: After the good cooperation with the specialist level, the impression has been imposed that "the head of the ministry is not behind the results of the scientific study". For the study, scientists from ten research institutions worked together in 23 subprojects.

title: According to the study, what is going on against racism in authorities?
embedding_text:
Title: According to the study, what is going on against racism in authorities?

Project manager Gert Pickel is disappointed: After the good cooperation with the specialist level, the impression has been imposed that "the head of the ministry is not behind the results of the scientific study". For the study, scientists from ten research institutions worked together in 23 subprojects. Depending on the subproject, quantitative and qualitative methods were used, including employee surveys, interviews, ethnographic observations as well as document and discourse analyses. Authorities at federal, state and local level were investigated. Jobcenter draws conclusions In the job center of the Hessian Main-Kinzig-Kreis, one takes the results of the study seriously, says board member Corinna Gessinger. "Of course, we are very busy and we are looking very closely at this topic."Since the flight movement in 2015/16, the Jobcenter has been focusing on intercultural training for all employees and is engaged in people with experience in flight who support translations, applications and advice. As a consequence of the InRa study, one wanted to improve communication in particular - for example, through a homepage in simple language, more understandable communications and digital applications, which can be completed in the respective mother tongue. However, Gessinger also emphasizes that his own consulting practice does not reflect the findings of the study to the same extent. Complaints with a racist background are the exception. The only case she knew from her term of office had turned out to be a communication problem. Often there is an impression of racist discrimination when discretional decisions are taken. While the principle is to treat the same facts equally - "but the facts are not always one to one", says the presidencies. The Ministry of the Interior speaks of "absolute exceptions" The Federal Ministry of the Interior (BMI) declares at the request of HR that the results of the InRa study would be submitted and discussed in the preparation of the National Action Plan against Racism. However, results are still pending. In addition, the Ministry refers to existing initiatives: "measures against discriminatory behaviour are firmly anchored in the BMI and its business area. They are already starting in training and are constantly being further developed," says one speaker. These included internal complaints bodies as well as the Federal Anti-Discrimination Office. In the future, the Federal Office for Migration and Refugees will, for example, be able to communicate reporting channels more clearly and involve external expertise more closely. At the same time, the BMI, the study confirms that the "large majority" of employees fulfil their tasks professionally and that discrimination remains "the absolute exception". Expert sees complaints as an opportunity Christa Kaletsch from the German Society for Democracy Education advises and trains authorities in dealing with racism. She stresses that many people would not trust or see any sense in making their stories public because of their experiences - "refuse, trivialization or, in the worst case, hostility or a perpetrator-victim-return". Some people don't even know that there's a chance to complain. She is convinced that the InRa study is an "epoche-making" study that must be dealt with. However, they do not see this attitude at present. "For our work, it would have been better if we had an attitude like 2020 when it came to saying that we have to deal with institutional racism." It would be a good start, says the free consultant, if complaints were understood not as an attack, but as an opportunity to improve our own work. It was not a question of calling employees racists on a flat-rate basis, but of questioning routines, laws and administrative procedures that penalise people. "Just get better" In recent years, there has been progress in the debate on institutional racism, says Kaletsch. For example, it is now able to use the term racism openly in training courses. In the past, the door was immediately closed. The recommendations for action of the InRa study are also taken into account in various places, according to study leader Pimpel: when drawing up the National Action Plan against Racism, for example, or in national and municipal processes. Nevertheless, one had to "just get even better", says consultant Kaletsch. Everything else is unworthy of a democratic society.

Succession of Spahn Merz Considers Cabinet Conversion For the successor of Union leader Spahn, Chancellor Merz can also imagine a remodelling of the government. Warken and Dobrindt in conversation The chair of the Union in the Bundestag is traditionally proposed by the two party leaders of CDU and CSU, who want to stay in discussion over the weekend. title: Why nurses in Australia are a dream job
embedding_text:
Title: Why nurses in Australia are a dream job

Labour market Care workers - a dream job in Australia While Germany is looking for nurses, the profession in Australia is in demand as rarely before - thanks to good pay, secure career prospects and social recognition. Patients taking showers, taking beds, losing blood: this is what a typical morning looks like for Nishaud Kuti at the hospital in Melbourne. The 23-year-old is still in the middle of his studies - in Australia it is part of doing various internships already during this time. For Nishaud, nurse has long been her dream job. "Because I want to take care of people. This is the standard answer, but I think it's true." If you have a great nurse in the hospital, you will always remember her, she is convinced. Influx of nursing programs In Australia, three years of nursing studies have been on the trend for years. According to current figures of the professional association for nurses from 2024, more than 28,000 young people have decided to study nursing care - almost 30 percent more than ten years earlier. At most universities there are now far more applicants than places of study. Care is particularly appreciated The great appreciation of the nursing profession in Australia makes it attractive for young people, says Tania Beament, deputy dean at the nursing school of Edith Cowan University in Perth. Since the Corona pandemic, this recognition has once again grown significantly. "In Australia, caring for the profession that people trust most. From birth to last breath - a caregiver is allowed to accompany a person on this whole journey of life. This is something very special," says Beament. The situation in Germany is quite different: surveys by professional associations repeatedly show that nursing staff in this country want much more recognition. Care professional among top earners The profession in Australia is also financially worthwhile. According to Annie Butler of the professional association, the average annual salary is about 60,000 euros. This makes nurses one of the top third of the country's income. Only about 30 percent of Australians earn as much or more. Supplements for additional qualifications are added: Those who have a master's degree, for example, receive a corresponding allowance. Fewer patients per nurse Another clear difference can be seen in the daily workload. According to the Federal Ministry of Health, while in Germany between ten and 20 patients come on a nurse depending on the department, according to Butler, there are only four in Australia. Nishaud Kuti also feels this in her internships: "If you need help, there are twelve people there and say: 'Of course, I'll help you.' That's really nice," she enthuses. Nevertheless, students in Australia are being prepared for high levels of stress in their everyday work, says Kara Bella Obbietivo, who studies at the University of Adelaide. "My class is about to graduate. We were told: We should be aware that while you are a caretaker, you have to pay attention to yourself," she says. Lack in the country Despite the demand for nursing courses, there are also bottlenecks in Australia, especially outside the big cities. This is confirmed by Olivia Gallagher, Head of the Department of Nursing at the University of Western Australia. In order to address this particular problem, her university has since this year established a new Bachelor of Nursing, which is targeted at rural areas. Students are enabled to learn in distance learning and to go through local practice stations. Australia's nursing studies are also becoming increasingly popular for international students. According to the Ministry of Education, the number of foreign students entering nursing care has more than doubled over the past ten years. One reason for this was a better chance of a residence permit. Nishaud Kuti from Melbourne has a year of study ahead of him. After that, she wants to specialize in intensive care and emergency care with a master's degree program - in order to start her dream job as a nurse.

title: Anger in Venezuela: "You built garbage!"

Title: Anger in Venezuela: "You built garbage!"

Anger in Venezuela "You've built garbage!" Many Venezuelans will probably never be able to rescue their relatives and friends from the earthquake ruins. All the more so is the anger at the government. In the face of the disaster, many now dare to openly criticise the government. "Let them all go to prison. You've built garbage!"Damely Díaz excites. She stands in front of the rubble of her apartment block in Catia La Mar and is upset. She confronts Nicolás Maduro Guerra, deputy of the ruling party and son of former president Nicolás Maduro, who was overthrown in January 2026 and has since been imprisoned in the USA. He was sanctioned by the US in 2019 because he was said to have enriched himself under his father. Díaz lost her daughter in the earthquake. In front of the camera, she blames the government for the collapse of the building. The scene, recorded by Norwegian TV2, is unusual for Venezuela: a government representative is publicly called up - and does not contradict. When asked by the Norwegian reporter whether he could understand the mother's anger, Maduro Guerra replied, "Yes, I understand her. I support them." Social housing in focus The mother's anger is directed against the social housing of the government. Many people in the state of La Guaira, severely hit by the double earthquakes, had already lost their homes with the mud avalanches in 1999 and had later moved into these state-built residential areas. Now some of them are facing nothing again. Katiuska Cartaya also said: "They gave us these apartments after our houses were completely destroyed. From the beginning, there were problems with the structure of the building." To this day, the program is considered one of the most important social projects of chavismism, founded by former President Hugo Chávez and then continued by his successor Nicolás Maduro. For years, however, there has been criticism of the quality of construction, maintenance and controls. Whether individual buildings have collapsed due to building defects has not yet been clearly clarified. The government is putting additional pressure on the fact that many of the Misión Vivienda's residential complexes were severely damaged or collapsed. Interim President Delcy Rodriguez has announced further measures for reconstruction. Together with private companies, new, earthquake-proof houses are to be built in the state of La Guaira. According to the state news agency AVN, there is also an export ban on building materials. In this way, the available materials for reconstruction should remain in the country. Thousands of dead, countless disappeared Around three weeks after the double quakes, the extent of the disaster is becoming more and more evident. According to official sources, more than 5,000 people were killed. How many people are still missing is not reported by the government. A privately set up database is based on about 30,000 missing persons.

title: Anger in Venezuela: "You built garbage!"
embedding_text:
Title: Anger in Venezuela: "You built garbage!"

According to official sources, more than 5,000 people were killed. How many people are still missing is not reported by the government. A privately set up database is based on about 30,000 missing persons. The La Esperanza cemetery in La Guaira is home to white crosses. Among many there is no name, but a QR code. It leads to the data of unidentified victims and is intended to help relatives find their family members later. For many families, the search for certainty is still not over. The houses built by the state were intended to reduce housing shortages in Catia La Mar. However, due to poor construction, they did not withstand the devastating earthquake. Fear of a life in the Provisional While relief organizations distribute food, drinking water and hygiene items, further shelters are being built in La Guaira. At the same time, there is growing concern among those concerned that a lasting solution could be made of the temporary arrangements. Numerous residential buildings are considered to be vulnerable to collapse after the earthquakes or have to be extensively renovated at first. In addition to food, people need psychological support, says Jonas Brenner of Caritas International. Many would have suffered trauma. The help is still needed for a long time. In part, people have lost everything: family, home, work Meanwhile, the government refers to the use of military and rescue forces and stresses that they have acted quickly. Residents, on the other hand, criticise that the help of their own government had been slow. This criticism is also substantiated after investigations by the non-governmental organization Transparencia Venezuela. It concludes that the Venezuelan authorities mobilized their search and rescue forces much more slowly than after comparable earthquakes in Chile, China or most recently in Turkey. According to the official figures evaluated, about 83 percent of the survivors were rescued from the immediate neighbourhood in the first few days, i.e. before the arrival of international aid. Freedom remains a demand of many Venezuelans. But after the earthquake, it is about daily survival - and the search for the victims. More Courage from Desperation Katiuska Cartaya also demands answers. "There are still so many missing people. They say they're in hospitals. But they can't be found there. Where have these people been? For us this means: Maybe they are still under the rubble of their houses." Ruins that are now being removed with heavy equipment. The disaster at Cartaya has changed something. She says she now has more courage to raise her voice. "I have more power now. We have a voice. We know our rights and we fight for them. We didn't do that before."

title: Spain: Large forest fire at Madrid out of control
embedding_text:
Title: Spain: Large forest fire at Madrid out of control

Spain Forest fire near Madrid is out of control

Northeast of Madrid, more than 500 forces on the ground and in the air are trying to contain a large forest fire. But the fire continues to spread. Several places had already to be evacuated.

Northeast of Spain's capital, Madrid, hundreds of forces are currently fighting a large forest fire. So far, they have tried in vain to control the fire. During the night on Sunday, it spread another 14 kilometers according to media reports. According to the authorities, about 13,000 hectares have already been burned in the affected area.

According to the data, more than 500 forces are currently working on fire-sniffs to contain the fire. So far, the regional minister for the environment, Mercedes Gómez, has managed to keep it away from towns. However, 20 municipalities should have been evacuated. In addition, 25 firefighting aircraft from the air tried to fight the fire, it was said.

Spain suffers from extreme drought

The fire broke out on Thursday in a wooded and mountainous area of the province of Guadalajara. It also raged in the Sierra Norte National Park, home to endangered species of eagles, wolves and butterflies.

Due to drought and low humidity, the risk of forest fire is currently extremely high in large parts of Spain. Since the beginning of the year, fires in the country have already destroyed more than 80,000 hectares of land. Last week, 13 people were killed in a fire in Andalusia.

title: Green boss Brantner: "Merz now has the chance to free herself"
embedding_text:
Title: Green boss Brantner: "Merz now has the chance to free herself"

Green boss Brantner in the ARD summer interview "Merz now has the chance to free herself"

Overdue, but also an opportunity: Green Chairman Franziska Brantner hopes for a cabinet conversion in the ARD summer interview after Spahn's resignation. And look with concern at the elections of the Landtag in autumn.

In the ARD summer interview, Green Chairman Franziska Brantner described Jens Spahn's resignation as overdue. Spahn has lost credibility in recent years, Brantner says. "It was the last drop that caused the barrel to overflow." The politician recalled the mask affair and also spoke of Spahn's proximity to Trump's "democracy destroyers."

Spahn had resigned after massive internal criticism of his family's founding by a surrogate mother abroad. A successor of Spahn is not yet known. However, Chancellor Friedrich Merz recently did not rule out a cabinet conversion.

Criticism of energy policy

"Merz now has the chance to free himself in the cabinet from all those who have manual weaknesses and are ideologically bored," Brantner says. That would be the moment to make tabula rasa and say, "We're going to go again."

Brantner criticized the German government's energy policy in particular. Minister of Economic Affairs Katherina Reiche does nothing but slow down renewable energies, says Brantner. Merz can now send a "stop sign".

Criticism of health reform

Brantner also criticised the Federal Government for, among other things, the recent health reform and warned against a hospital death. In the case of inheritance tax, there were gaps in justice, for example, in the inheritance of housing units.

Although it defended the Greens' support for easing the debt brake last year, it limited it: "It makes me angry and sometimes stunned, which the government does with these funds. That's not acceptable."

Moreover, it was not appropriate to continue to make debts. "In the field of defence, we firmly believe that this must eventually be financed again from the core budget," says Brantner. "We cannot in the long term finance our security through debt to the next generations."

"In the end, it's really about the sausage"

In the federal elections in the eastern German federal states, the Greens tend to be much weaker than in the western federal states. Most recently, the Sunday question in Mecklenburg-Vorpommern was 5 percent, in Saxony-Anhalt only 4 percent. Addressed at the upcoming state elections in Saxony-Anhalt and Mecklenburg-Western Pomerania in autumn, Brantner admitted: "People are not standing on the edge with the sunflower and are waiting for us."

For the elections in the East, according to Brantner, the Greens have now mobilized the entire party. She warned against development and insisted that the Greens move into the Landtag. "In the end, it's really about sausage." There's a danger that the AfD will rule alone in Saxony-Anhalt, says Brantner. "We really give everything because it's about so much."

Addressing her own - clearly weaker - reputation values as her predecessor with the Greens, she said: "I am certainly not the one who is the shrillest. I am not the one who promises everything to everyone, as Mr Merz did." But she also does not believe that it is necessary in these times.
